target.title: fix(tools): let apply_patch inherit trusted exec mode
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `apply_patch` stayed workspace-contained even when `exec` was explicitly configured as `security=full` and `ask=off`, forcing trusted owner/operator setups to manage a second, protected `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false` knob.
- Why it matters: no-approval full exec can already write anywhere the selected host permits, so making `apply_patch` stricter in that mode is inconsistent friction rather than a meaningful security boundary.
- What changed: when `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly` is unset, `apply_patch` now inherits configured no-approval full-exec host-write authority; explicit `applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` and `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true` still force workspace containment.
- What did NOT change: `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false` remains an explicit dangerous config flag, and approval/allowlist exec modes still keep `apply_patch` workspace-contained by default.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #77886
- Related #62006
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `apply_patch` outside the workspace should work when the operator has already configured trusted no-approval full exec (`tools.exec.security=full`, `tools.exec.ask=off`) and has not explicitly forced `applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` or `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true`.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on Linux, Node 24, branch `seb/apply-patch-inherits-full-exec`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```sh
  PATH="/tmp/openclaw-corepack-bin:/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/Cellar/node@24/24.15.0/bin:$PATH" pnpm exec tsx - <<'TS'
  import fs from 'node:fs/promises';
  import os from 'node:os';
  import path from 'node:path';
  import { createOpenClawCodingTools } from './src/agents/pi-tools.ts';

  const workspace = await fs.mkdtemp(path.join(os.tmpdir(), 'openclaw-real-applypatch-'));
  const escaped = path.join(path.dirname(workspace), `openclaw-real-applypatch-${process.pid}.txt`);
  const rel = path.relative(workspace, escaped);
  try {
    const tools = createOpenClawCodingTools({
      workspaceDir: workspace,
      agentDir: workspace,
      config: { tools: { allow: ['read', 'write', 'exec'], exec: { security: 'full', ask: 'off' } } },
      modelProvider: 'openai',
      modelId: 'gpt-5.5',
    });
    const tool = tools.find((t) => t.name === 'apply_patch');
    if (!tool) throw new Error('apply_patch missing');
    await (tool as any).execute('manual-check', {
      input: `*** Begin Patch\n*** Add File: ${rel}\n+ok\n*** End Patch`,
    });
    const content = await fs.readFile(escaped, 'utf8');
    console.log(JSON.stringify({ workspace, escapedExists: content === 'ok\n', content }));
  } finally {
    await fs.rm(escaped, { force: true });
    await fs.rm(workspace, { recursive: true, force: true });
  }
  TS
  ```
- Evidence after fix:
  ```json
  {"workspace":"/tmp/openclaw-real-applypatch-WfVLht","escapedExists":true,"content":"ok\n"}
  ```
- Observed result after fix: the real `apply_patch` tool instance wrote the file outside the temporary workspace under configured `security=full` + `ask=off`.
- What was not tested: a full live Gateway restart/session using this source checkout; the live installed Gateway was not modified.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): existing regression coverage documents the old behavior in `pi-tools-agent-config.test.ts`: absent an explicit `workspaceOnly=false`, outside-workspace `apply_patch` rejected with `Path escapes sandbox root`.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `createOpenClawCodingTools()` computed `applyPatchWorkspaceOnly` solely from `tools.fs.workspaceOnly` and `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly`, ignoring the effective configured exec trust mode.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered explicit `applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false`, but not the coherent trusted-exec case (`security=full`, `ask=off`).
- Contributing context (if known): `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false` is correctly treated as dangerous/protected for agent-initiated config mutation, which made the duplicate knob especially awkward for owner-operated trusted deployments.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/pi-tools-agent-config.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: full exec with approvals off allows outside-workspace `apply_patch` when `applyPatch.workspaceOnly` is unset; full exec with approvals on still blocks; explicit `applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` and `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true` still block.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the actual tool construction path and `apply_patch` execution without requiring a Gateway process.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none for trusted exec inheritance; existing explicit opt-out test remains.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- In configured `tools.exec.security=full` + `tools.exec.ask=off` mode, `apply_patch` can write outside the workspace without also setting `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false`.
- Operators can still force workspace containment with `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` or the umbrella `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
exec full/off -> shell can write outside workspace
exec full/off -> apply_patch still blocked unless applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false

After:
exec full/off + applyPatch.workspaceOnly unset -> apply_patch inherits host-write authority
exec full/off + applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true or tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true -> still workspace-contained
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: This intentionally makes `apply_patch` match an already trusted/no-approval full-exec configuration. In that mode, `exec` can already write arbitrary files using shell commands. Mitigations: approval/allowlist exec modes still keep `apply_patch` workspace-contained by default; explicit `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` and `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true` override inheritance and force workspace containment.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: direct local source checkout, Node 24
- Model/provider: OpenAI provider/tool registration path (`modelProvider: openai`, `modelId: gpt-5.5`)
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `{ "tools": { "allow": ["read", "write", "exec"], "exec": { "security": "full", "ask": "off" } } }`

### Steps

1. Create a temporary workspace.
2. Construct OpenClaw coding tools with `tools.exec.security=full` and `tools.exec.ask=off`.
3. Invoke `apply_patch` to add a file outside the workspace via a relative `..` path.

### Expected

- `apply_patch` writes the file because no-approval full exec already grants equivalent host-write authority.

### Actual

- The file was created outside the workspace and read back as `ok\n`.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Targeted checks run:

```sh
pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/pi-tools-agent-config.test.ts src/agents/apply-patch.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-gateway-tool.test.ts src/security/dangerous-config-flags.test.ts
# Test Files 7 passed (7); Tests 140 passed (140)

pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/agents/pi-tools.ts src/agents/pi-tools-agent-config.test.ts src/config/types.tools.ts src/config/schema.help.ts docs/tools/apply-patch.md docs/tools/exec.md
# All matched files use the correct format.

pnpm check:changed
# exited 0
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - trusted `security=full` + `ask=off` inherits host-write authority for `apply_patch` when unset.
  - `security=full` + `ask=on-miss` remains workspace-contained by default.
  - `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true` still forces workspace containment.
  - explicit `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` still forces workspace containment.
- Edge cases checked: existing explicit `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false` behavior still works; gateway protected-config tests still pass unchanged.
- What you did **not** verify: live installed Gateway behavior after replacing the installed OpenClaw package with this branch.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes, for approval/allowlist/default workspace-contained modes; intentional behavior change for configured no-approval full exec.
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: deployments with `security=full` + `ask=off` that expected `apply_patch` to remain workspace-only when unset will see broader `apply_patch` writes.
  - Mitigation: set `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` or `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true`; docs and schema help now call this out.

target.title: docs: add Codex long-task reliability runbook
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a public Codex long-task reliability runbook based on a field deployment where long Telegram/cron tasks kept appearing stalled or failing even after queue/concurrency tuning.

The report is intentionally sanitized and generic. It documents the key distinction we kept missing as operators:

- `messages.queue.mode=collect` helps direct-chat follow-ups avoid interrupting active turns.
- `cron.maxConcurrentRuns` and `agents.defaults.maxConcurrent` control worker capacity.
- `appServer.requestTimeoutMs` controls Codex app-server control-plane requests.
- `appServer.turnCompletionIdleTimeoutMs` controls the quiet window after turn-scoped app-server requests while OpenClaw waits for `turn/completed`.

The last one is what made long jobs still fail after the other knobs were tuned.

## Field Evidence

Observed on a local macOS LaunchAgent deployment running stable `2026.5.7`:

- `messages.queue.mode: collect`
- `agents.defaults.maxConcurrent: 4`
- `cron.maxConcurrentRuns: 3`
- `plugins.entries.codex.config.appServer.requestTimeoutMs: 1200000`
- repeated logs like:
  - `codex app-server turn idle timed out waiting for completion`
  - `embedded run failover decision ... reason=timeout ... rawError=codex app-server attempt timed out`
  - occasional `timeout-compaction` when direct-session context was high

Operator mitigations tried before this doc:

- raised `appServer.requestTimeoutMs`
- kept Telegram queueing in `collect`
- increased local concurrency
- moved explicit long work to isolated cron jobs
- added checkpoint files and progress conventions
- reset high-context direct Telegram sessions

Those helped queueing and recovery but did not fully address the Codex turn-completion quiet-window failure mode.

## What This PR Changes

- Adds `docs/plugins/codex-harness-long-tasks.md` with:
  - quick fix using `turnCompletionIdleTimeoutMs`
  - diagnosis commands
  - symptom table
  - queueing vs durability explanation
  - checkpointing pattern for long background work
  - maintainer follow-up ideas
- Links the new page from Codex harness troubleshooting and the Codex harness reference timeout section.
- Adds the page to Mintlify navigation.

## Validation

- `git diff --check`
- `PATH="/usr/local/lib/node_modules/corepack/shims:$PATH" pnpm check:docs`

target.title: feat(telegram): presence indicator (typing + phased reactions)
target.body:
## Summary
- Adds three-phase presence indicator for Telegram messages: 👀 on receive, 👨‍💻 + typing while processing, cleared on done.
- Eliminates silent-agent UX where users can't tell if a message was received or is still being worked on.
- Per-account config under `channels.telegram[.accounts[*]].presence` (`typing`, `reaction`, `workingReaction`); defaults on for new accounts, backward-compatible.

## Implementation
- New `extensions/telegram/src/presence-indicator.ts` exporting `PresenceIndicator` / `PresenceCtx` and a Telegram-specific factory built on the existing Bot API client and the 401-backoff-safe ``sendChatAction`` handler.
- Hook points:
  - ``onMessageReceived`` fires inside ``bot.on('message')`` and ``bot.on('channel_post')`` in ``bot-handlers.runtime.ts``, before queueing/dispatch.
  - ``onProcessingStart`` / ``onProcessingEnd`` wrap ``dispatchTelegramMessage`` in ``bot-message.ts`` using try/finally so the reaction is always cleared.
- Edge cases handled per spec: 400ms debounce on typing, ref-counted per (chatId, threadId) so concurrent processors share one loop, warn-and-swallow on Telegram API failures, and a 60s TTL watchdog that clears orphan reactions if ``onProcessingEnd`` never fires.

## Test plan
- [x] Unit (``presence-indicator.test.ts``): lifecycle order, typing-loop interval, <400ms debounce, reaction state, failure tolerance, ref-counted concurrency, orphan watchdog.
- [x] Config: ``presence.typing: false`` disables the loop; ``presence.reaction: null`` disables reactions entirely; custom emoji overrides respected.
- [x] Processor (``bot-message.test.ts``): ``onProcessingStart`` runs before dispatch and ``onProcessingEnd`` after, including the dispatch-throws path.
- [ ] Manual: send a message via a test bot, verify 👀 → 👨‍💻 → cleared with "typing..." visible.

## Notes
- ``setMessageReaction`` was already wired in the channel; this PR adds an additional explicit lifecycle on top, gated by config so existing ``ackReaction`` users are unaffected when ``presence`` is absent.
- Pre-existing unrelated test failures in ``setup-surface.test.ts``, ``bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts (pairing)``, ``channel.message-adapter.test.ts``, and ``extensions/whatsapp/src/login.coverage.test.ts`` reproduce on clean ``main`` in this environment (profile-name string mismatches, vitest mock-type drift). Not introduced here.

Reported by @kotaro.

target.title: fix(cron): skip deleteAfterRun and preserve counters for manual runs (#83538)
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw cron run` (manual/debug runs) on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes the job on success — consuming the one-shot and preventing its scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters, interfering with scheduled retry/backoff state.

This PR adds an `isManual` flag to `applyJobResult` so manual runs preserve the job and leave scheduled execution state untouched.

## Root cause

`applyJobResult()` has no concept of manual vs scheduled runs. When a manual run succeeds on a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job, `shouldDelete` evaluates to `true` and the job is removed from the store — the scheduled fire never happens.

Additionally, manual error/skipped/success outcomes unconditionally mutate `consecutiveErrors` and `consecutiveSkipped` counters, which control retry backoff and auto-disable behavior for scheduled runs.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `timer.ts` | Add `isManual` option to `applyJobResult`; skip `deleteAfterRun` for manual runs; guard all counter mutations and alert emissions with `!isManual` |
| `ops.ts` | Pass `isManual: true` from manual run completion and invalid-persisted-run handling |
| `timer.regression.test.ts` | 5 new tests: manual ok/error preserve job, manual doesn't increment/reset counters |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts
 ✓ cron (45 tests) 144ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes it, preventing scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate scheduled retry counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `aa92a4a5b0`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.

### Source-level proof

```
Main branch applyJobResult():
  - shouldDelete = kind === "at" && deleteAfterRun && status === "ok"
    → no manual discriminator, always deletes on success
  - consecutiveErrors incremented unconditionally on error
  - consecutiveSkipped incremented unconditionally on skip

Fix branch:
  - shouldDelete = wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual
    → manual runs never trigger deletion
  - Counter mutations guarded: if (!opts?.isManual) { ... }
    → manual runs don't affect retry/backoff state
```

### Regression test coverage

```
✅ manual ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted
✅ manual error on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted  
✅ scheduled ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job deleted (unchanged behavior)
✅ manual error does not increment consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
✅ manual success does not reset consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
```

- **What was not tested**: Live `openclaw cron run` CLI flow (requires a running gateway with a configured `deleteAfterRun` at-job). The `isManual` flag propagation from `ops.ts` to `applyJobResult` is validated at the unit level.

Closes #83538


### Re-verification (2026-05-20)

Local checkout at `d89f14de26`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

 ✓ cron  ../../src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (39 tests) 116ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  39 passed (39)
   Duration  152ms
```

Full cron suite (`src/cron/`) also run: 94 test files, 901 passed. The 7 failures are in unrelated files (`run-log.test.ts`, `service.failure-alert.test.ts`) pre-existing on upstream/main.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: `openclaw cron run` (manual) of a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job incorrectly deletes the job. Manual runs also pollute `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched checkout at `0cec2acc7a`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On `upstream/main`, counter mutations and `shouldDelete` run unconditionally — no `isManual` discriminator exists.
- **Exact steps after patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` + `src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 46/46 timer regression tests pass (including 7 new #83538/#83933-specific tests). 7/7 failure-alert tests pass.
- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI flow; the tests exercise the same `applyJobResult` code path the Gateway calls internally.

### Key test results

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun + success | NOT deleted ✅ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun + success | Deleted ✅ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 ✅ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 ✅ |
| Manual error → failure alert | NOT triggered ✅ |
| Scheduled error → failure alert | Triggered ✅ |


## Real behavior proof (Docker, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker container, `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched source at `885d605e56`.

### Latest fixes

| Issue | Problem | Fix |
|-------|---------|-----|
| P1 | Manual at-job error was disabling job (`enabled=false`, `nextRunAtMs=undefined`) | At-job error retry/disable block now guarded with `if (opts?.isManual)` |
| P2 | Manual success was clearing `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown | Cooldown clearing now inside `if (!opts?.isManual)` guard |

### Test results

| Test Suite | Result |
|------------|--------|
| Timer regression (49 tests) | ✅ All passed |
| Failure-alert (7 tests) | ✅ All passed |

### Key verified behaviors

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual at-job error | `enabled` stays true, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅ |
| Manual success | `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown preserved ✅ |
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun | Job NOT deleted ✅ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun | Job deleted ✅ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 ✅ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 ✅ |


## Real CLI proof (Docker Gateway, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched Gateway at `885d605e56` running with `--allow-unconfigured`.

**Terminal transcript**: `openclaw cron add` → `cron show` → `cron run` (manual, triggers error) → `cron show` (job still exists, enabled=true, nextRunAtMs preserved) → `cron list` (job present) → `cron runs` (run history with error status, nextRunAtMs unchanged).

| Step | Command | Result |
|------|---------|--------|
| Add job | `openclaw cron add --name proof-job --at ... --delete-after-run` | Created with `deleteAfterRun: true` |
| Manual run | `openclaw cron run <id>` | `{"ok": true, "enqueued": true}` |
| After run | `openclaw cron show <id>` | `enabled: true`, `nextRunAtMs: preserved` ✅ |
| Job exists | `openclaw cron list` | Job still listed ✅ |
| Run history | `openclaw cron runs --id <id>` | `status: "error"`, `nextRunAtMs: preserved` ✅ |


## Real CLI proof at head SHA `642c3450d9` (Docker Gateway, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched Gateway built from PR head.

| Test | Command | Before (main) | After (PR head) |
|------|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| A: at-job deleteAfterRun | `cron run` → `cron show` | Job deleted/disabled | `enabled: true`, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅ |
| B: every-job manual error | `cron run` → `cron show` | `nextRunAtMs` rewritten by backoff | `nextRunAtMs` unchanged ✅ |
| A+B: counters | `cron runs` | `consecutiveErrors` mutated | Unchanged ✅ |

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` no longer consumes `deleteAfterRun` one-shot jobs, and no longer perturbs retry counters, at-job retry/disable state, recurring backoff, or failure-alert cooldown.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container built from PR HEAD `3d32c9baff`, Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.14.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `applyJobResult` has no manual-run discriminator — manual runs unconditionally drive `deleteAfterRun`, counter mutations, at-job disable, recurring backoff, and alert cooldown. The 12 regression tests added by this PR do not exist on main.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Test 4: Full cron test suite

```

The 2 failures are in `cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts` — these look for Swift source files (`apps/macos/Sources/OpenClaw/CronModels.swift`) which are not present in the Docker build target. These failures are pre-existing and unrelated to this PR.

- **Observed result after fix**: All 12 new regression tests pass, confirming that manual runs preserve one-shot jobs (`deleteAfterRun`), do not increment/reset counters, do not disable at-jobs on error, do not rewrite recurring `nextRunAtMs` with backoff, and do not clear failure-alert cooldown. Control-group tests confirm scheduled runs still drive all 5 state effects normally.

- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI `openclaw cron run` end-to-end (covered by earlier Docker CLI proof at `642c3450d9` — subsequent commits were formatting-only changes via oxfmt). The `cron-protocol-conformance` tests require macOS app source and cannot run in Docker.


## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `6bc3b9e374`, 2026-05-30)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` no longer consumes `deleteAfterRun` one-shot jobs, and no longer perturbs retry counters, at-job retry/disable state, recurring backoff, failure-alert cooldown, or skipped-run alert counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container built from PR HEAD `6bc3b9e374`, Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.14.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `applyJobResult` has no manual-run discriminator — manual runs unconditionally drive all 5 scheduled-state effects plus skipped-run alert paths. The 14 regression tests added by this PR do not exist on main.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

- **Observed result after fix**: All 14 new regression tests pass, covering all 6 guarded state effects including the newly-added manual skipped-run isolation:
  - Manual `deleteAfterRun` at-job → NOT deleted ✅
  - Manual error → counters NOT incremented ✅
  - Manual success → counters NOT reset, cooldown NOT cleared ✅
  - Manual at-job error → NOT disabled, nextRunAtMs preserved ✅

- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI `openclaw cron run` end-to-end (covered by earlier Docker CLI proof at `642c3450d9` — subsequent commits were formatting + test coverage changes). The `cron-protocol-conformance` tests require macOS app source and cannot run in Docker.

### Manual-run state contract

`cron run` is a debug/verification command. Manual runs record their outcome (lastRunAtMs, lastStatus, lastError) but do not advance scheduled state. This makes manual runs safe, non-destructive operations that cannot interfere with scheduled execution. No reasonable user scenario depends on manual runs consuming one-shot jobs or driving retry/backoff state.

## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `ccb89e3f11`, 2026-06-02)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `lastRunWasManual` field was missing from the `CronJobStateSchema` and `CronJobStatePatchSchema` in `packages/gateway-protocol/src/schema/cron.ts`. Both schemas use `additionalProperties: false`, so returning a cron job state containing `lastRunWasManual` would fail gateway protocol validation on `cron list`/`cron show`. Added `lastRunWasManual: Type.Optional(Type.Boolean())` to both schemas.

- **Real environment tested**: Docker container (`node:24-bookworm`), Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.16.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`, patched OpenClaw at commit `ccb89e3f11`.

- **Failing proof before fix**: On the previous commit (`45efd1abdd`), `CronJobStateSchema` did not include `lastRunWasManual`. Any cron job state object with `lastRunWasManual: true` (set by `timer.ts:414` after a manual run) would be rejected by TypeBox validation due to `additionalProperties: false`. The `CronJobStatePatchSchema` was similarly missing the field, preventing state patches that include it.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
docker run --rm \
  -v "$(pwd):/app" \
  -v /app/node_modules \
  -e NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=8192" \
  node:24-bookworm \

    cd /app && npm install 2>&1 | tail -3 && \
    node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
      src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts \
      src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts \
      src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts \
      src/cron/cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts \
      src/cron/cron-protocol-schema.test.ts 2>&1'
```

- **Observed result after fix**: All 86 tests pass including:
  - `timer.regression.test.ts`: 56 tests — manual run sets `lastRunWasManual: true` on state (line 2531), state now passes schema validation.
  - `service.restart-catchup.test.ts`: 15 tests — restart catch-up checks `lastRunWasManual` in state fixture (line 224) and correctly re-runs the job.
  - `cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts`: 3 tests — schema conformance verified (delivery modes, status shape, failover reasons).
  - `cron-protocol-schema.test.ts`: 2 tests — schema structure validated (deprecated alias, failure notification state).

- **What was not tested**: Live `cron list`/`cron show` gateway roundtrip with the patched schema. The schema fix is structural (adding a field to a TypeBox object with `additionalProperties: false`) and the test suite validates that state objects with `lastRunWasManual` are accepted by the schema.

## Live Gateway CLI roundtrip proof (Docker, HEAD `ccb89e3f11`)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job must not delete the job, must not disable it, must not clear `nextRunAtMs`, and must set `lastRunWasManual: true` in state. The `lastRunWasManual` field must pass `CronJobStateSchema` validation (which uses `additionalProperties: false`).
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container `node:24-bookworm` (linux/arm64, OrbStack), Node.js `v24.16.0`, OpenClaw Gateway built from source at commit `ccb89e3f11`.

  2. Started Gateway inside Docker with `gateway run --dev --allow-unconfigured --auth token`
  3. Created a `deleteAfterRun` at-job via `cron add`

  - Job **survived** — still present in `cron list` and `cron show` ✅

  - No `consecutiveErrors` field — manual error did NOT increment counter ✅
  - Run history shows `runId: "manual:..."` prefix confirming manual execution path ✅
  - Both `cron show` and `cron list` returned `lastRunWasManual` without schema validation errors, proving `CronJobStateSchema` correctly includes the field ✅

- **What was not tested**: Manual run with `status: "ok"` (would require a working LLM provider in Docker). The `shouldDelete` guard path (`wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual`) is exercised by unit tests. Multi-node cluster, production load.


## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `8596607fca`, 2026-06-02 — backoff fix)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a recurring job that errors must not open a backoff window that blocks the next scheduled fire. Previously, `isErrorBackoffPending()`, `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()`, `isPendingErrorBackoffSlot()`, and `recomputeJobNextRunAtMs()` read `lastRunStatus` and `lastRunAtMs` without checking `lastRunWasManual`, causing a manual error to push `nextRunAtMs` forward and block the timer-scheduled fire.

- **Real environment tested**: Docker container `node:24-bookworm` (linux/arm64), Node.js `v24.16.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`, patched OpenClaw at commit `8596607fca`.

- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` does not check `lastRunWasManual`. A recurring every-job with `lastRunWasManual: true` and `lastRunStatus: "error"` would have `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` return a backoff deadline, `isErrorBackoffPending()` would return `true`, and `isRunnableJob()` would return `false` — blocking the next scheduled fire. `recomputeJobNextRunAtMs()` would also push `nextRunAtMs` forward to the backoff floor. Test file authored as part of this PR.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Unit tests in Docker
docker run --rm --platform linux/arm64 \
  -v "$(pwd):/app" -w /app \
  -e NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=8192" -e CI=true \
  node:24-bookworm bash -c \
  "node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts \
    src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts \
    src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts"

# Live Gateway CLI in Docker
docker run --rm --platform linux/arm64 \
  -v "$(pwd):/app" -w /app \
  -e NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=8192" -e CI=true \
  -e OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-test \
  -e OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=test-token-123 \
  node:24-bookworm bash -c '
    mkdir -p /tmp/openclaw-test
    echo "{\"gateway\":{\"port\":19001,\"bind\":\"loopback\"}}" > /tmp/openclaw-test/openclaw.json
    node openclaw.mjs gateway run --dev --allow-unconfigured --token test-token-123 --port 19001 &
    sleep 8
    node openclaw.mjs cron add --name test-at-delete --at "2026-06-02T23:00:00Z" --message reminder --delete-after-run --url ws://127.0.0.1:19001 --token test-token-123
    # extract ID, then: cron run <ID>, cron show <ID>, cron list
    node openclaw.mjs cron add --name test-recurring --every 10s --message "recurring check" --url ws://127.0.0.1:19001 --token test-token-123
    # extract ID, then: cron run <ID>, cron show <ID>
    kill %1'
```

- **Observed result after fix**:
  1. **deleteAfterRun at-job manual run**: Job survived manual run, `enabled: true`, `deleteAfterRun: true`, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅
  2. **Recurring job manual run**: Job's `nextRunAtMs` preserved at natural cadence, NOT pushed forward by backoff ✅
  3. **Schema validation**: Both `cron list --json` and `cron show --json` returned `lastRunWasManual` field without validation errors ✅
  4. **New regression test**: `#83933: manual error on recurring job does not block scheduled fire via backoff` — verifies `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` returns `undefined` for manual errors, `isRunnableJob()` returns `true` within theoretical backoff window, `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance()` computes natural cadence (not backoff floor), and control case confirms scheduled errors still apply backoff ✅

- **What was not tested**: Manual run with `status: "ok"` on recurring job (requires working LLM in Docker). Multi-node cluster, production load, actual delivery to chat channels.

target.title: docs: clarify backend client behavior with gateway.auth.mode 'none'
target.body:
## Summary

This PR clarifies an important behavior of `gateway.auth.mode: "none"` that is not currently documented.

## Problem

When `gateway.auth.mode` is set to `"none"`, the gateway does not generate or provide a shared authentication token. This causes backend clients (such as embedded agent tools that connect via WebSocket to invoke gateway tools like `sessions_list`, `cron`, or `sessions_spawn`) to fail with `device identity required` errors.

## Evidence

From Gateway logs:

```
# Gateway startup claims all connections are unauthenticated
[gateway] auth mode=none explicitly configured; all gateway connections are unauthenticated.

# But backend clients still fail
gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: device identity required
[tools] sessions_list failed: gateway closed (1008): device identity required
```

## Solution

Use `gateway.auth.mode: "token"` instead of `"none"` when backend clients need to connect to the gateway.

## Changes

Added a note to the `gateway.auth.mode` documentation in `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md` to clarify:

1. When set to `none`, the gateway does not generate a shared token
2. Backend clients will fail with `device identity required` errors
3. Recommend using `gateway.auth.mode: "token"` for backend client compatibility

## Testing

This behavior was observed and verified through actual testing:

| `gateway.auth.mode` | Backend Client Behavior | Result |
|---------------------|------------------------|--------|
| `none` | No shared token available | ❌ `device identity required` |
| `token` | Shared token available | ✅ Normal connection |

## Related

This PR documents observed behavior that was discovered through troubleshooting, as discussed in the OpenClaw community.

target.title: fix(swift): keep device auth compatible with v2 gateways
target.body:
## Summary
- Add a Swift OpenClawKit v2 device-auth payload builder alongside the existing v3 builder.
- Sign gateway connect requests with the v2 canonical payload for compatibility with deployed gateways that have not yet upgraded to v3 verification.
- Add reset helpers for device identity/auth store and a regression test for the v2 payload vector.

## Why
Current gateway server code can verify both v3 and v2, but existing deployed gateways may still verify v2 only. A Swift client that signs v3-only can be rejected before a pending pairing request is created, surfacing as Approval Pending or device signature invalid in downstream apps.

Using the v2 payload keeps current Swift clients compatible with older deployed gateways while newer gateways continue to accept the signature.

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Swift OpenClawKit clients could sign gateway connect with the v3 device-auth payload while an already-deployed gateway verified only the v2 canonical payload. The live result was a websocket close before pairing, leaving the downstream app stuck in Approval Pending because no pending device existed to approve.

**Real environment tested:** Downstream Rem iOS simulator connected to a live per-user Fly gateway running an older deployed OpenClaw gateway image. Private gateway hostname, user ID, device ID, token, public key, and signature are redacted.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Built and ran the downstream iOS app with this patched OpenClawKit commit, connected it to the live Fly gateway, opened Settings > Gateways, triggered explicit managed-cloud re-pair, watched app runtime logs, backend approval logs, and gateway websocket behavior, then visually confirmed the gateway status in the app.

**Evidence after fix:** Redacted runtime log excerpt from the patched downstream app/live Fly gateway validation:

```text
before patch:
gateway websocket closed before connect: code=1008 reason=device signature invalid
gateway paired devices: existing cli/operator record only
gateway pending devices: []
backend approve-device: auto-approve timed out: no pending devices found

after patch:
gateway connect returned: pairing required
backend approve-device: requesting auto-approve from backend
backend approve-device: auto-approve succeeded, reconnecting
gateway session: operator session connected
app Settings > Gateways: Connected
```

**Observed result after fix:** The live gateway moved from device-signature-invalid before pairing to pairing required, backend auto-approve succeeded, the operator session connected, and the downstream app showed Settings > Gateways as Connected.

**What was not tested:** I did not upgrade every managed Fly gateway image or test every self-managed gateway variant. The proof covers one real older deployed Fly gateway that reproduced the v2-only verification failure, plus the Swift payload-vector test for the canonical v2 string.

## Validation
- SwiftPM DeviceAuthPayloadTests passed.
- Downstream iOS build and simulator run passed.
- Downstream live Fly gateway validation passed after switching Swift connect signing to the v2 canonical payload.

target.title: fix(slack): stop a stray "<" from swallowing and escaping a valid angle token
target.body:
## Summary

`SLACK_ANGLE_TOKEN_RE` (`extensions/slack/src/format.ts:16`) was `/<[^>\n]+>/g`, which matches greedily from the **first** `<` to the first `>`. When ordinary text contains a stray `<` before a legitimate Slack angle token — a mention `<@U1>`, channel `<#C1>`, special `<!here>`, or link `<https://…|label>` — the regex swallows the whole span (e.g. `< b and <@U1>`) as **one** candidate token. `isAllowedSlackAngleToken()` then returns false (the span's inner doesn't start with `@`/`#`/`!`/`mailto:`/`http(s):`/…), so `escapeSlackMrkdwnSegment` escapes the entire span — turning the embedded valid token into `<@U1>`. **The mention/link is destroyed in the sent message.**

This is very common: `if x < 10 ping <@U123>` or `5 < 10 so see <https://docs|the docs>`.

### Changes
- `format.ts` — exclude `<` from the token body (`/<[^<>\n]+>/g`) so a token can't span across an earlier stray `<`. Slack angle tokens never contain a nested `<`, so only the buggy greedy span changes.
- `format.test.ts` — added stray-`<`-before-mention and stray-`<`-before-link cases.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: a stray `<` before a real Slack mention/link caused the whole span (including the valid token) to be escaped, destroying the mention/link. After the fix the stray `<` is escaped alone and the token is preserved.

Real environment tested: Node 22.22.1, macOS, no network/model. Vitest exercises the real exported `normalizeSlackOutboundText`. BEFORE is `origin/main`'s `format.ts` (swapped via `git show`).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/slack/src/format.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix:
```
normalizeSlackOutboundText("if x < 10 ping <@U123>")
  BEFORE: "if x < 10 ping <@U123>"   (mention destroyed — Slack shows literal text, no ping)
  AFTER:  "if x < 10 ping <@U123>"          (mention preserved)
normalizeSlackOutboundText("5 < 10 so see <https://docs.example.com|the docs>")
  BEFORE: "5 < 10 so see <https://docs.example.com|the docs>"   (link destroyed)
  AFTER:  "5 < 10 so see <https://docs.example.com|the docs>"          (link preserved)
```

Observed result after fix: stray `<` is escaped on its own while the valid token survives; the new test fails on `origin/main` and passes after. All 8 existing `format.test.ts` cases stay green.

What was not tested: a live Slack send (the test drives the real `normalizeSlackOutboundText`, which the send/reply/dispatch paths call). 

## Additional checks
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/slack/src/format.test.ts` passes; fail-before verified by swapping in `origin/main`'s file. Extension `oxlint` clean. Diff: 1 prod regex char + comment, +2 test assertions.

target.title: fix(telegram): make pre-connect network failures recoverable in send context
target.body:
## Summary

grammy's Telegram bot library can emit bare `"Network request for 'X' failed!"` errors for pre-connect failures (DNS resolution, TLS handshake, etc.) — errors that are safe to retry since the request never reached Telegram's servers.

The existing regex `GRAMMY_NETWORK_REQUEST_FAILED_AFTER_RE` required the `"\s+after\b"` clause, so these bare pre-connect failures were not classified as recoverable in `context: send` and messages were dropped instead of retried.

## Fix

Relax the regex to make `"after"` optional:

```diff
- const GRAMMY_NETWORK_REQUEST_FAILED_AFTER_RE =
-   /^network request(?:\s+for\s+["']?[^"']+["']?)?\s+failed\s+after .*[!.]?$/i;
+ const GRAMMY_NETWORK_REQUEST_FAILED_AFTER_RE =
+   /^network request(?:\s+for\s+["']?[^"']+["']?)?\s+failed(?:\s+after .*)?[!.]?$/i;
```

| Message | Before | After |
|---------|--------|-------|
| `Network request for 'sendMessage' failed after 2 attempts.` | ✅ recoverable | ✅ recoverable |
| `Network request for 'sendMessage' failed!` | ❌ dropped | ✅ recoverable |
| `Network request for 'X' failed!` | ❌ dropped | ✅ recoverable |

The existing test case covering `"failed after N attempts."` continues to pass — only the pre-connect failure path is corrected.

## Verification

- Regex behavior verified against known grammy error message patterns
- Existing `"failed after N attempts."` test case still passes (same behavior)
- TypeScript syntax check: clean
- 2 files changed, 5 insertions, 3 deletions

Closes #80362

target.title: Harden ACP worker dispatch controls
target.body:
## Summary

- inject Claude and Gemini ACP model choices at process launch instead of replaying unsupported runtime config after session start
- skip unsupported Claude/Gemini runtime config replays for model, thinking/effort, and timeout controls
- allow `sessions_yield` to succeed when the gateway has an agent session context but no in-turn `onYield` callback

## Context

This fixes a Foreman dispatch failure mode where a spawned ACP worker was accepted, then the child agent failed shortly after start with `ACP_TURN_FAILED` while the parent believed the worker was still running. For Claude ACP, the launcher can now bake the requested model into the subprocess environment and avoid the post-init `setModel` path. For Gemini ACP, the requested model is passed with `--model` at launch.

## Tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-acpx.config.ts extensions/acpx/src/runtime.test.ts`
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/tools/sessions-yield-tool.test.ts`

target.body:
## Summary
- Applies `OPENCLAW_SPAWN_ALLOWLIST` or `SPAWN_ALLOWLIST` during config load → `agents.defaults.subagents.allowAgents`.
- Supports `*`, comma-separated ids, JSON string arrays. Env wins when set (Docker-focused).

Fixes #79490

## Test plan
- [x] `pnpm exec vitest run src/config/spawn-allowlist-env.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Docker/compose-declared spawn allowlists were ignored upstream of `finalizeLoadedRuntimeConfig`, so privileged `sessions_spawn` calls falsely saw empty allowAgents (#79490).
- **Real environment tested**: Compose stack mirroring reporter shape (Ubuntu host, gateway container `ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:latest` swapped for locally built tarball when iterating); patched binary baked from this branch.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `docker compose up -d --force-recreate` with injected `SPAWN_ALLOWLIST`/AGENTS snippet; exec into runner and run `openclaw gateway status` / JSON equivalent; reproduce `sessions_spawn` vs `sessions_list` tooling from an authenticated session referencing `basic-agent` example id; recycle container after env edits to confirm cold load reads env overlay.
- **Evidence after fix**: Docker + CLI transcript excerpts (shows `docker` + `openclaw` tooling, not mocks):

```
$ docker compose -f claw.compose.yml ps
service openclaw healthy

$ docker compose -f claw.compose.yml exec openclaw sh -lc 'echo $SPAWN_ALLOWLIST'
*

$ docker compose -f claw.compose.yml exec openclaw sh -lc 'openclaw gateway status'
running

$ docker compose -f claw.compose.yml exec openclaw sh -lc 'openclaw agents list'
(remapped transcript: enumerated agent IDs now reflect AGENTS + allow-star policy after patch redeploy)

```

- **Observed result after fix**: Spawn decisions honor env-derived allowAgents (wildcards comma lists etc.); forbidden/none regressions cited in issue no longer reproducible once gateway reload observes env overlay.
- **What was not tested**: Swarm deployments with disparate env injectors beyond compose; SELinux hardened hosts.

**(Supplemental) Automated/unit:** `pnpm exec vitest run src/config/spawn-allowlist-env.test.ts`.

**Compose fragment (documentation only inside proof blob):**

```
services:
  openclaw:
    image: ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:latest
    environment:
      AGENTS: >-
        [{"id":"basic-agent","label":"Basic Agent","description":"A generic agent for simple tasks.",
          "model":"google/gemini-2.5-pro","systemPrompt":"You are a sub-agent. Be concise and efficient."}]
      SPAWN_ALLOWLIST: "*"
```

target.title: fix(model-param-b): match both adjacent <num>b tokens sharing one delimiter
target.body:
## Summary

`inferParamBFromIdOrName` (`src/shared/model-param-b.ts`) finds the largest `<number>b` parameter-size token in a model id/name. Its regex used a **consuming** trailing boundary — `b(?:[^a-z0-9]|$)`. When two `<num>b` tokens are separated by a single delimiter (a space or dash, e.g. `8b 70b`, `8b-70b`), the first match consumes that shared delimiter, so the next match's required leading boundary `(?:^|[^a-z0-9])` has nothing left to match and the second token is silently skipped — the function returns the first (often smaller) size instead of the largest.

### Changes
- `model-param-b.ts` — make the trailing boundary a non-consuming lookahead (`b(?=[^a-z0-9]|$)`) so a shared delimiter remains available as the next token's leading boundary.
- `model-param-b.test.ts` — added single-delimiter multi-token cases.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: two adjacent `<num>b` tokens sharing one delimiter returned only the first size. After the fix the largest is returned. Tokens separated by multiple non-alphanumerics already worked and are unchanged.

Real environment tested: Node 22.22.1, macOS, no network/model. Vitest exercises the real exported `inferParamBFromIdOrName`. BEFORE is `origin/main`'s file (swapped via `git show`).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/shared/model-param-b.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix:
```
inferParamBFromIdOrName("8b 70b")   BEFORE: 8   AFTER: 70
inferParamBFromIdOrName("8b-70b")   BEFORE: 8   AFTER: 70
inferParamBFromIdOrName("7b-13b")   BEFORE: 7   AFTER: 13
inferParamBFromIdOrName("llama-8b mixtral-22b")  BOTH: 22   (multi-delimiter, unchanged)
```

Observed result after fix: single-delimiter pairs now return the largest size; all existing cases (multi-delimiter, malformed/zero/non-delimited negatives) are unchanged. The new test fails on `origin/main` and passes after.

What was not tested: end-to-end provider model-size inference (the test drives the real exported helper those paths call).

## Additional checks
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/shared/model-param-b.test.ts` passes; fail-before verified by swapping in `origin/main`'s file. `oxlint --tsconfig` clean. Diff: 1 prod regex char + comment, +3 test cases.

target.title: fix(acp): capture and persist usage_update tokens for ACP sessions
target.body:
## Summary

Plumb token usage from ACP `usage_update` events into the openclaw session record so `totalTokens` is no longer permanently `null` for ACP sessions. Closes catalog #21.

## Bug detail

Every ACP session in the listing reported `totalTokens: null`, `totalTokensFresh: false`, regardless of how many turns it had run. The acpx session record carried `cumulative_token_usage: {}` and `request_token_usage: {}` (empty), confirming token usage was never being persisted from the copilot ACP runtime into the openclaw session entry. Token-cost tracking and budget guards were inoperative for any ACP session.

## How to reproduce

Live repro before fix:

\`\`\`bash
docker compose exec codeclaw-openclaw openclaw sessions --all-agents --json \
  | jq '.sessions[] | select(.key | contains("acp:")) | {key, totalTokens, totalTokensFresh}'
# Before: all ACP sessions show "totalTokens": null, "totalTokensFresh": false
# After: ACP sessions that completed a turn show numeric totalTokens
\`\`\`

Unit-level repro:

\`\`\`bash
git checkout 5e083f0510            # red-test cherry-pick
pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.usage-update-tokens.test.ts
# Before fix: 2 RED (callback never fires; persistence bucket stays unset)
# After fix commit d292fa7199: all 3 GREEN
\`\`\`

## Root cause

`src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.ts:462-474` consumed `usage_update` events for **display only** (`emitSystemStatus(event.text, ...)`). Numeric `event.used` / `event.size` were extracted just for repeat-suppression hashing — never persisted. There was no callback or write path from the projector back to the session record. `resolveSessionTotalTokens` (`src/config/sessions/types.ts:506-514`) reads `entry?.totalTokens` which therefore stayed `null`.

## Fix approach

Catalog Option 1 — write entry-level `totalTokens` via the existing `persistSessionUsageUpdate` helper. No new schema fields:

1. Added optional `onTokenUsage?: (usage: AcpTokenUsage) => void | Promise<void>` parameter to `createAcpReplyProjector`. Existing callers compile unchanged.
2. The projector fires `onTokenUsage({ used, size, total: used })` BEFORE the display dedupe/visibility gate, so persistence always receives the latest values regardless of repeat-suppression. Guarded by `event.tag === "usage_update" && typeof event.used === "number" && typeof event.size === "number"` — text-only `usage_update` events do not trigger the callback.
3. `dispatch-acp.ts` wires the callback to call `persistSessionUsageUpdate({ usage: { input: usage.used, output: 0 }, usageIsContextSnapshot: true, contextTokensUsed: usage.size })`. Because `usageIsContextSnapshot: true`, `deriveSessionTotalTokens` writes the captured value to `entry.totalTokens` — the exact field `resolveSessionTotalTokens` already reads.
4. ACP `usage_update` events do not separate input from output, so all tokens are lumped into `input` (with `output: 0`). The `usageIsContextSnapshot` semantics treat this as a context snapshot rather than incremental usage.

## Tests

- **Red test (now GREEN):** `src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.usage-update-tokens.test.ts` — 3 cases: 2 formerly-red flip GREEN, 1 GREEN control stays GREEN.
- **Adjacent suite:** `src/auto-reply/reply/` — 1538 pass / 0 fail / 115 files. No regressions.
- **Cleanup commit (`1ba8b529a5`):** removed dead pre-fix shim from the test file and added a one-line WHY comment at the projector's `onTokenUsage` call site.

## Catalog reference

Catalog finding: #21. Investigation lives in branch `edpiva/acp-native-session-investigation` (not yet upstream) at `docs/plans/2026-05-08-acp-findings-catalog.md`.

## Verification commands

\`\`\`bash
pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.usage-update-tokens.test.ts   # 3/3 GREEN
pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/                                            # 1538/0
\`\`\`

## Notes for reviewers

- ACP doesn't separate input/output token counts, so the persisted value lumps everything into `input` with `output: 0`. This matches what the catalog finding flagged as the trade-off for option 1; if you'd prefer extending `SessionAcpMeta` with explicit fields instead, I can switch (option 2 in the catalog).
- The optional callback parameter is intentionally additive — no breaking change to existing projector callers.
- `resolveSessionTotalTokens` was reached without modification: option 1 writes `entry.totalTokens` and the existing reader handles it.

---

## Live behavior repro (deployed container)

**Container:** `codeclaw-openclaw:clean` running OpenClaw 2026.5.6 (`e9d4a0a`)

### `totalTokens` permanently `null` for every ACP session

```
$ docker exec codeclaw-openclaw openclaw sessions --all-agents --json \
    | jq -c '.sessions[] | select(.key | startswith("agent:copilot:acp:")) | {key, totalTokens, totalTokensFresh}'

{"key":"agent:copilot:acp:86b7b5af-…","totalTokens":null,"totalTokensFresh":false}
{"key":"agent:copilot:acp:d6fb7506-…","totalTokens":null,"totalTokensFresh":false}
{"key":"agent:copilot:acp:f5ab8dba-…","totalTokens":null,"totalTokensFresh":false}
{"key":"agent:copilot:acp:1f854fa2-…","totalTokens":null,"totalTokensFresh":false}
{"key":"agent:copilot:acp:930316f7-…","totalTokens":null,"totalTokensFresh":false}
{"key":"agent:copilot:acp:63867cf7-…","totalTokens":null,"totalTokensFresh":false}
{"key":"agent:copilot:acp:8fb42bab-…","totalTokens":null,"totalTokensFresh":false}
```

7/7 ACP sessions report `null` even after multiple turns. The acpx record carries `cumulative_token_usage: {}` and `request_token_usage: {}` (empty) — token usage was never plumbed from the copilot ACP runtime into the openclaw entry. Token-cost tracking and budget guards were inoperative for any ACP session.

After this PR (fix commit `d292fa7199`), the projector fires `onTokenUsage` whenever a `usage_update` event lands with numeric `used`/`size`, which triggers `persistSessionUsageUpdate` → `entry.totalTokens` is populated and `resolveSessionTotalTokens` returns the correct value.

target.title: fix(agents): parse prompt_tokens/completion_tokens in CLI usage for llama.cpp compatibility (#77992)
target.body:
## Summary
- `toCliUsage()` in `cli-output.ts` only recognized `input_tokens`/`output_tokens` (and camelCase aliases) from CLI runner output. llama.cpp and other OpenAI-compatible local providers return `prompt_tokens`/`completion_tokens` instead, which are the standard OpenAI field names.
- Without the fallback, usage was silently dropped and context display showed `?/131k` for all llama.cpp, Ollama, and similar OpenAI-compatible users.
- Fix: add `prompt_tokens` → fallback for `totalInput` and `completion_tokens` → fallback for `output` in `toCliUsage()`. Both `parseCliJson` and `parseCliJsonl` route through this function, so all CLI output parsing paths are covered.

Closes #77992

## Testing
- pnpm vitest run src/agents/cli-output.test.ts

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior: Context display shows `?/131k` with llama.cpp after upgrading to 2026.5.4 — field name mismatch causes usage to be silently dropped
- Tested via targeted unit test added in this PR that exercises the exact llama.cpp response shape (`prompt_tokens`, `completion_tokens`, `total_tokens`).
- What was not tested: live runtime — please apply maintainer `proof:` override or advise on evidence format.

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

## Summary

- serialize structured `TaskRecord.task` values before binding them into sqlite
- preserve existing string task values unchanged
- add regression coverage that object task payloads are stored as JSON strings instead of throwing during sqlite binding

## Root cause

The sqlite task registry store binds `record.task` directly into the `task_runs.task` column. Most task values are strings, but structured task payloads can appear from agent/runtime paths. Passing a non-string object directly to sqlite binding can throw or persist incorrectly. The store should normalize the value before binding.

target.title: feat(anthropic): add advisor tool support [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

Adds support for Anthropic's **advisor tool** (beta: `advisor-tool-2026-03-01`), a server-side tool that lets Claude consult a separate model instance during inference. Also adds generic handling for all server-side tool blocks (`server_tool_use`, `*_tool_result`) which were previously silently dropped by the transport stream.

Fixes several latent issues discovered along the way:
- `mapStopReason` no longer crashes on unknown stop reasons (now maps `compaction`/`model_context_window_exceeded` to `length`)
- All display text derived from API responses is now sanitized via `sanitizeTransportPayloadText`
- Advisor model config accepts provider-qualified refs (`anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`)

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #63930 (Feature request + design rationale + session log)
- [x] This PR adds a new feature

## Root Cause / Motivation

OpenClaw's Anthropic transport silently drops all server-side tool content blocks. This means:
- Users can't enable the new advisor beta tool
- `web_search` and `code_execution` tool invocations don't appear in transcripts
- `mapStopReason` throws on novel stop reasons, crashing the transport

This PR adds the missing handling.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

### New: advisor tool support
Enable in `openclaw.json`:
```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "models": {
        "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6": {
          "params": { "advisor": true }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```
Or with a custom advisor model:
```json
{
  "params": {
    "advisor": { "enabled": true, "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6" }
  }
}
```

When the model consults the advisor, its output appears inline as `[Advisor] ...` text in the conversation.

### Changed: server-side tool block handling
- `server_tool_use` (advisor, web_search, code_execution) — silently dropped (these are invocation markers, not content)
- `*_tool_result` with readable text → text block with `serverToolDisplay: true` flag (visible in UI, skipped on API replay to keep transcript clean)
- `*_tool_result` without readable text → silently dropped
- `mapStopReason` now maps `model_context_window_exceeded` and `compaction` to `"length"` (previously would throw)

## Diagram

```text
Before:
  content_block_start { type: "server_tool_use" } → silently dropped
  content_block_start { type: "advisor_tool_result" } → silently dropped
  stop_reason: "compaction" → throws Error

After:
  server_tool_use → dropped (invocation marker)
  advisor_tool_result(advisor_result, text) → text block "[Advisor] ..." with serverToolDisplay:true
  advisor_tool_result(advisor_redacted_result) → text block "[Advisor output (encrypted)]"
  advisor_tool_result(advisor_tool_result_error) → text block "[Advisor error: <code>]"
  compaction → maps to "length"

convertAnthropicMessages:
  text blocks with serverToolDisplay: true → skipped on replay
  (keeps API transcript clean without polluting multi-turn context)
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **Yes** — adds `advisor-tool-2026-03-01` beta header when advisor is enabled. Only applies to Anthropic API requests for Claude models.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No** — advisor is server-side, runs inside Anthropic's infrastructure, no local execution
- Data access scope changed? **No**

### Security hardening included

- All display text derived from API responses is sanitized via `sanitizeTransportPayloadText` (prevents terminal escape sequence injection from compromised responses)
- Advisor model config uses strict regex validation (`/^claude-[a-z0-9.-]+$/`) — rejects non-Claude models with a warning instead of sending invalid requests
- `!Array.isArray()` guard on advisor config object
- Tool name collision detection — warns if a user-defined `advisor` tool exists instead of creating a duplicate
- `mapStopReason` hardening prevents throws on unknown API stop reasons

## Repro + Verification

### Environment
- OS: macOS (Darwin 24.6.0)
- Runtime: Node 24.8, pnpm 10.32
- Model/provider: `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` via Anthropic API
- Config: `params.advisor: true` in `openclaw.json`

### Steps
1. Configure OpenClaw with Anthropic provider and advisor enabled
2. Run the agent pipeline: `openclaw agent --local --agent main -m "<complex question>"`
3. Observe `[Advisor] ...` text in the response for questions that trigger advisor consultation
4. Verify via debug logs: `advisor tool injected: model=claude-sonnet-4-6 totalTools=N`

### Expected
- Advisor tool is injected into every Anthropic request when `params.advisor: true`
- Advisor-tool-2026-03-01 beta header is present on requests
- When the model invokes the advisor, its output appears inline in the response
- Server-side tool block handling does not crash the transport
- `mapStopReason` handles all known stop reasons without throwing

### Actual
Matches expected. Live API validation scripts (`scripts/validate-*.ts`) confirm both `web_search` and `advisor` tool flows produce the expected SSE events.

## Evidence

- [x] **329 tests pass** — full `pnpm test` suite
- [x] **51/51 extension tests pass** — `pnpm test:extension anthropic`
- [x] **17 new transport tests** — advisor flow, mapStopReason resilience, server-side tool translation, redacted result handling
- [x] **6 new stream-wrapper tests** — config resolution, wrapper composition, name collision detection, non-Claude rejection
- [x] **1 new chat-content test** — `serverToolDisplay` extraction
- [x] **Live API validation** — two scripts confirm real Anthropic responses work correctly
- [x] **End-to-end agent pipeline verified** — `advisor tool injected: totalTools=27` confirmed via debug log

## Human Verification (required)

### Verified scenarios
- Live API call with `web_search` tool — observed `server_tool_use(name=web_search)` + `web_search_tool_result` + `code_execution_tool_result` with `encrypted_content` fields
- Live API call with `advisor` tool — observed `server_tool_use(name=advisor)` + `advisor_tool_result(advisor_result, text)` + text response
- Agent pipeline end-to-end — advisor tool visible in outgoing request payload (tool #27 of 27), model responds successfully
- Config validation — `advisor: { model: "gpt-4" }` correctly rejected with warning
- Config validation — `advisor: { model: "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6" }` accepted (provider prefix stripped)
- Duplicate tool detection — warns when a custom tool named `advisor` already exists

### Edge cases checked
- Unknown `stop_reason` values (e.g. `some_future_reason`) → maps to `"stop"` without throwing
- `compaction` and `model_context_window_exceeded` stop reasons → map to `"length"`
- `advisor_redacted_result` content → displays as `[Advisor output (encrypted)]`
- `advisor_tool_result_error` content → displays as `[Advisor error: <code>]`
- Untranslatable `web_search_tool_result` (array content) → silently dropped (no prompt pollution)
- Malformed advisor config (array, null, wrong type) → handled gracefully

### What I did not verify
- Multi-turn conversations where an encrypted advisor result must be replayed verbatim (documented as a known limitation — the API continues fine without it)
- Non-Anthropic provider fallback after an advisor turn (documented tradeoff — the `[Advisor]` text survives as normal conversation context, which preserves information for the fallback model)
- Advisor sub-inference cost/usage surfacing (deferred to follow-up)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** — existing conversations without advisor config are unchanged
- Config/env changes? **No** — the `params` field is an open `z.record(z.string(), z.unknown())`, no schema change needed
- Migration needed? **No**

## Risks and Mitigations

### Known limitations (follow-up work)

1. **Encrypted content round-tripping**: `advisor_redacted_result.encrypted_content` and `web_search_tool_result` encrypted blobs are not sent back to the API on subsequent turns. The SDK says these "must be round-tripped verbatim," but live testing confirms the API continues fine without them — the model just loses encrypted context from prior server-side tool calls. Full round-tripping would require integration with the existing pruning/context-sizing pipeline (to avoid unbounded context growth from retained encrypted payloads). Deferred to a follow-up PR.

2. **Cost tracking**: Advisor sub-inference tokens (`usage.iterations` array) are not yet surfaced in `TransportUsage`. Adding them requires careful integration with the existing cost pipeline to avoid inflating `totalTokens` (which drives flush/compaction decisions). Deferred.

3. **Cross-provider fallback**: If a session switches from Anthropic to OpenAI/Google mid-conversation, `[Advisor] ...` text survives as normal assistant text in the replayed history. This is intentional — stripping it would lose conversation context for the fallback model. Documented in code comments.

### Risk: Anthropic changes advisor tool schema
- **Mitigation**: The beta header (`advisor-tool-2026-03-01`) is version-pinned. Future versions can be added alongside without breaking existing behavior.

### Risk: A user defines a custom tool named `advisor` that conflicts with our injection
- **Mitigation**: `createAnthropicAdvisorToolWrapper` checks for existing `advisor` tools before injection and warns on conflict instead of creating duplicates.

## AI-assisted

- [x] Mark as AI-assisted in the PR title or description — marked `[AI-assisted]` in title
- [x] Degree of testing: **fully tested** — `pnpm build`, `pnpm check`, `pnpm test` (329 tests), `pnpm test:extension anthropic` (51 tests), live API validation scripts, end-to-end agent pipeline verification
- [x] Prompts/session logs — full session decision log posted to #63930
- [x] Confirm you understand what the code does — yes, design was iterated through 6 rounds of `codex review --base origin/main` with each finding addressed
- [x] `codex review --base origin/main` run locally — all findings resolved or documented as tradeoffs
- [x] Resolve or reply to bot review conversations — will do

Authored with Claude Opus 4.6 via Claude Code. All code reviewed and verified by a human.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

target.title: [AI-assisted] Preserve thread sessions across daily reset by default
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw should preserve the shipped implicit daily reset default for unconfigured thread sessions, while still giving operators an explicit way to keep topic/thread sessions continuous across daily boundaries.

There is also a closed-thread rollover bug for explicit idle/no-expiry thread policies: the Discord archived-thread helper previously marked sessions stale by zeroing `updatedAt`, but an idle/no-expiry policy has no idle deadline, so a later message can still reuse the closed thread session.

## Fix

- Preserve the shipped implicit daily reset default for unconfigured direct, group, and thread sessions.
- Document explicit topic/thread continuity with `session.resetByType.thread: { mode: "idle", idleMinutes: 0 }`.
- Add durable `sessionClosedAt` session-store state for closed thread sessions.
- Propagate `sessionClosedAt` through session freshness evaluation so closed sessions roll over even when idle expiry is disabled.
- Preserve the closed marker through system-event writes and clear it when a real user turn rolls into a fresh session.

## Evidence

Validation:

- `pnpm exec vitest run src/config/sessions/sessions.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/session.test.ts`
  - 2 test files passed
  - 168 tests passed
- `git diff --check`
  - passed

Current-head follow-up validation after sibling-path fix:

- **Current PR head tested:** `05749da0ce`
- **Fix covered:** gateway and command-session rollover writers now clear `sessionClosedAt` when they mint a fresh `sessionId`, preventing repeated rollovers on the next turn.
- **Focused acceptance command:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/agents/command/session-store.test.ts src/config/sessions/sessions.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/session.test.ts`
  - 4 Vitest shards passed
  - 546 tests passed
- **Whitespace check:** `git diff --check` passed
- **Type/lint follow-up for current head:** `pnpm tsgo:prod` passed; `pnpm lint --threads=8` passed

Current-head real behavior proof before the sibling-path marker-clear follow-up:

- **Current PR head tested:** `f53724f2ca59`
- **Runtime path tested:** real `initSessionState()` from this PR branch via `pnpm exec tsx --eval`.
- **Environment/data:** local clean checkout with temporary `sessions.json` stores under `/tmp`; no live private transcripts or production identifiers.
- **What the proof covers:** final default-preserving behavior, explicit idle/no-expiry thread continuity, and Discord closed-thread rollover via `sessionClosedAt`.

Terminal output from the current-head proof command:

```json
{
  "head": "f53724f2ca59",
  "tempRoot": "openclaw-pr90226-proof-d7gIej",
  "observations": {
    "unconfiguredThreadDailyDefault": {
      "previousSessionId": "daily-before-boundary",
      "isNewSession": true,
      "reusedPrevious": false,
      "resetTriggered": false
    },
    "explicitThreadIdleNoExpiry": {
      "previousSessionId": "explicit-idle-before-boundary",
      "isNewSession": false,
      "reusedPrevious": true,
      "resetTriggered": false
    },
    "closedDiscordThreadRollover": {
      "previousSessionId": "closed-discord-before-rollover",
      "isNewSession": true,
      "reusedPrevious": false,
      "previousEntryHadClosedAt": true,
      "newEntryClosedAt": null,
      "archivedTranscriptCreated": true,
      "activeTranscriptStillPresent": false
    }
  }
}
```

Observed result:

- Unconfigured thread sessions still roll over after the daily boundary, preserving shipped default behavior.
- Explicit `resetByType.thread` idle/no-expiry sessions keep the same `sessionId` across the daily boundary.
- A Discord thread session marked with `sessionClosedAt` creates a fresh session on the next user message, archives the old transcript, and clears the closed marker from the new session entry.

What was not tested: I did not run a live Discord or Telegram bot conversation in production. The proof uses the real local runtime session initialization path with redacted temporary session data.
