target.title: feat(config-ui): add basic/advanced field split and doc-link affordance
target.body:
● - [x] Add FIELD_ADVANCED set in schema.hints.ts to mark technical config fields (auth cooldowns, gateway infra knobs, diagnostics subsections, etc.) as advanced, so the config form can hide
   them by default
  - [x] Add docLink field to ConfigUiHint type for future per-field docs links
  - [x] In config-form.node.ts renderObject, split top-level section fields into basic (visible by default) and advanced (collapsed <details> disclosure) when no search is active; search
  bypasses the split so all matching fields are always visible
  - [x] Add renderHelpBlock helper that renders help text, doc link, and tags together; render the Docs ↗ anchor when hints.docLink is set
  - [x] Add CSS for .cfg-advanced-section disclosure and .cfg-field__doc-link

  ## Summary

  - **Problem:** The config UI shows all settings at the same level — basic toggles sit alongside low-level infra knobs (auth backoff timers, gateway health-check intervals, otel settings),
  making the page feel dense and hard to navigate for non-expert users.
  - **Why it matters:** New users opening the config page see 50+ fields at once with no visual hierarchy; important settings like log level or update channel are buried next to
  rarely-touched tuning parameters.
  - **What changed:** Top-level fields in each config section are now split into "basic" (always visible) and "advanced" (collapsed `<details>` disclosure with a count badge). The split is
  bypassed when a search query is active so all matching fields remain discoverable. The `ConfigUiHint` type gains an optional `docLink` field; when set, a `Docs ↗` anchor is rendered inline
  with the help text.
  - **What did NOT change:** Raw mode, search behavior, the Form/Raw toggle, nested-object collapsibles, tag filtering (`tag:advanced`), and plugin-owned channel hints are all untouched.

  ## Change Type (select all)

  - [ ] Bug fix
  - [x] Feature
  - [ ] Refactor required for the fix
  - [ ] Docs
  - [ ] Security hardening
  - [ ] Chore/infra

  ## Scope (select all touched areas)

  - [ ] Gateway / orchestration
  - [ ] Skills / tool execution
  - [ ] Auth / tokens
  - [ ] Memory / storage
  - [ ] Integrations
  - [ ] API / contracts
  - [x] UI / DX
  - [ ] CI/CD / infra

  ## Linked Issue/PR

  - Closes #75947
  - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

  ## Root Cause (if applicable)

  N/A

  ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

  N/A — feature addition, not a regression fix.

  - Coverage level that should have caught this:
    - [ ] Unit test
    - [ ] Seam / integration test
    - [ ] End-to-end test
    - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient
  - Target test or file: `src/config/schema.hints.test.ts` (42 tests pass, hint building logic unchanged)
  - Scenario the test should lock in: N/A
  - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: N/A
  - Existing test that already covers this (if any): `src/config/schema.hints.test.ts`
  - If no new test is added, why not: The change is a pure rendering split in Lit templates; the split logic (`hintForPath(...).advanced`) is trivial and covered indirectly by existing
  hint-building tests.

  ## User-visible / Behavior Changes

  - Config page sections now show only "basic" fields by default; technical/tuning fields are hidden inside a collapsed "Advanced" disclosure with a count badge (e.g. "Advanced 5").
  - Clicking the disclosure reveals the advanced fields in-place, consistent with existing nested-object collapsibles.
  - Searching (`tag:advanced`, free text) bypasses the split — all matching fields appear regardless of their audience tier.
  - No default values, keys, or config behavior changed.

  ## Diagram (if applicable)

  ```text
  Before:
  [Auth section] -> [profiles] [order] [cooldowns.*] [backoff timers...] (all flat)

  After:
  [Auth section] -> [profiles] [order]
                    ▸ Advanced (1)  <- collapsed by default
                      └─ [cooldowns.*] [backoff timers...]

  Search active:
  [Auth section] -> all matching fields visible regardless of advanced flag

  Security Impact (required)

  - New permissions/capabilities? No
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
  - New/changed network calls? No
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? No
  - Data access scope changed? No

  Repro + Verification

  Environment

  - OS: Linux (Fedora 43)
  - Runtime/container: Node 22, local dev
  - Model/provider: N/A
  - Integration/channel: N/A
  - Relevant config: default

  Steps

  1. Open the control UI → Yapılandırma (Configuration) tab
  2. Navigate to the Authentication section
  3. Observe that "Auth Profiles" and "Auth Profile Order" are visible by default; "Auth Cooldowns" is collapsed under "Advanced (1)"
  4. Click "Advanced" disclosure → cooldown fields appear
  5. Type tag:advanced in the search box → all advanced fields across all sections appear

  Expected

  - Basic fields visible, advanced fields collapsed by default
  - Search bypasses the split and shows all matches

  Actual

  - Matches expected behavior

  Evidence

  - Failing test/log before + passing after — pnpm test src/config/schema.hints.test.ts: 42 passed

  Human Verification (required)

  - Verified scenarios: Basic/advanced split renders correctly; search overrides split; Form/Raw toggle unaffected; dark/light theme looks correct
  - Edge cases checked: Section with zero advanced fields (no disclosure rendered); search with tag:advanced returns advanced fields across all sections
  - What you did not verify: Full E2E in all locales; mobile layout

  Review Conversations

  - I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
  - I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

  Compatibility / Migration

  - Backward compatible? Yes
  - Config/env changes? No
  - Migration needed? No

  Risks and Mitigations

  - Risk: A field that should be "basic" ends up in FIELD_ADVANCED (or vice versa), hiding something a user needs to find.
    - Mitigation: The search box always reveals all fields regardless of the split; no field is permanently hidden. The FIELD_ADVANCED set is a small explicit allowlist — only paths we
  deliberately audited are marked.

target.title: fix(video-generation): bound dashscope task response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`src/video-generation/dashscope-compatible.ts` reads two provider-controlled
response bodies (the submit-task response and the poll-status response) with
raw `await response.json()` calls. That call has no byte cap, so a faulty or
hostile DashScope endpoint streaming an unbounded body can force the runtime
to buffer the whole payload before parsing — an OOM / DoS surface in the
production path. The download path on line 338 already uses
`readResponseWithLimit`; this brings the submit and poll paths into the
same bound-read family.

The shared `readProviderJsonResponse` helper in
`openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http` (used by the bound-stream family merged
in #95218, #95417, #95418, #95420, #95246, #96136) caps the read at 16 MiB
and surfaces an actionable error before OOM. This PR routes both the
submit and poll DashScope paths through that helper.

## Why This Change Was Made

- Cap is symmetric with the download path already in the same file.
- Bounded-read helper is generic, owned by `provider-http`, and already
  proven on `provider-http-errors.ts` and the image-generation/video-
  generation sibling surfaces.
- Single-file source change + regression tests, low review surface, no
  public API change.

## Changes

- `src/video-generation/dashscope-compatible.ts` — `+11/-2` — import
  `readProviderJsonResponse` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`;
  replace two raw `await response.json()` calls (line 203, the poll-status
  path, and line 273, the submit-task path) with
  `readProviderJsonResponse<DashscopeVideoGenerationResponse>(response,
  label)`.
- `src/video-generation/dashscope-compatible.bounded-json.test.ts` —
  `+181/-0` — new test file. 4 unit tests covering: well-formed poll
  parse, overflow on the poll path (stops at 17/32 chunks for a 32 MiB
  body), well-formed submit path parse, **and** overflow on the submit
  path via `runDashscopeVideoGenerationTask` (directly exercises the
  submit function ClawSweeper's previous review flagged as "not
  directly proven").
- `scripts/repro/issue-dashscope-response-cap.mjs` — `+114/-0` — new
  real-environment repro driving the production
  `pollDashscopeVideoTaskUntilComplete` with a 32 MiB streaming body
  and proving the bounded reader stops at 17/32 chunks. Includes a
  **negative control** showing that raw `response.json()` on the same
  body buffers the full 32 MiB before failing on parse.

## Evidence

### Real-environment repro output

```
$ pnpm exec tsx scripts/repro/issue-dashscope-response-cap.mjs
=== Reproduction for dashscope-compatible bounded JSON read ===
PROVIDER_JSON_RESPONSE_MAX_BYTES = 16777216 bytes

PASS  bounded read: threw "dashscope video-generation task poll: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes" after 17 / 32 chunks (stopped ~17 MiB into a 32 MiB body)
PASS  negative control: raw response.json() buffered all 2 / 2 chunks (2 MiB) and failed only on JSON parse — proves bounded read is the right shape

=== All repro assertions passed ===
```

### Unit tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/video-generation/dashscope-compatible.bounded-json.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
```

The 4th test ("surfaces a verbatim bounded-reader error from the submit
path") calls `runDashscopeVideoGenerationTask` end-to-end with a 100
MiB streaming body on the submit response and asserts the bounded
reader throws with the canonical overflow error and stops pulling
chunks well before the body completes.

## ClawSweeper finding addressed in this revision

The previous review's blocker said "the branch is not merge-ready
because it misses the current provider-http test mock contract and
does not actually exercise the submit helper it changes."

- Submit helper is now directly exercised by the new submit-path test
  that calls `runDashscopeVideoGenerationTask` and asserts the
  verbatim overflow error from the bounded reader.
- The bounded reader is the same one exposed via
  `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http` (`readProviderJsonResponse`),
  so the test exercises the canonical helper, not a local copy.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/video-generation/dashscope-compatible.bounded-json.test.ts`
  — 4/4 passed
- `pnpm exec tsx scripts/repro/issue-dashscope-response-cap.mjs` — 2/2 PASS
- `npx oxlint --import-plugin --config .oxlintrc.json` on the 3 changed
  files — exit 0
- Rebased onto current `openclaw/main` (`4d034639ad`); no merge
  conflicts.


## CI status note

The most recent run reported one failing check
(`checks-node-compact-large-1` /
`src/agents/session-write-lock.test.ts` /
`acquireSessionWriteLock > retries when a stale lock report is
replaced by a fresh payload-less lock`). This failure is in the
session write-lock retry path, which shares no surface with the
video-generation change in this PR. The PR only touches
`src/video-generation/dashscope-compatible.ts` and its test/repro
files; the failing test exercises `acquireSessionWriteLock` in
`src/agents/session-write-lock.ts`, an unrelated module. A re-run
should clear it.

## Out of scope

- Sibling surfaces in `src/agents/chutes-oauth.ts` (4 sites),
  `src/infra/clawhub.ts` (3 sites), and others are intentionally
  left for follow-up PRs.
- The `readResponseWithLimit` download path is already bounded in this
  file and does not need changes.

target.title: fix(googlechat): preserve unresolved service account refs
target.body:
## Summary
- Preserve unresolved Google Chat `serviceAccountRef` as an error instead of silently falling back to legacy inline `serviceAccount` credentials.
- Add regression coverage for mixed `serviceAccountRef` + inline `serviceAccount` config.

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Google Chat credential resolution must honor documented SecretRef precedence. A config with both `serviceAccountRef` and inline `serviceAccount` must fail closed when the ref is unresolved, and a resolved runtime snapshot must remove the sibling ref before Google Chat reads credentials.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw PR worktree on the rebased head `ef2bfcd48f`, using Node/pnpm from the repo environment. I checked env and `~/.profile` for Google Chat service-account material without printing values; none was present, so this uses redacted service-account JSON through the production Google Chat account resolver plus runtime SecretRef assignment/resolution paths.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `pnpm exec tsx` against `extensions/googlechat/src/accounts.ts` and `extensions/googlechat/src/secret-contract.ts`, invoking `resolveGoogleChatAccount`, `collectRuntimeConfigAssignments`, `resolveSecretRefValues`, and `applyResolvedAssignments` with a mixed ref-plus-inline Google Chat config and a resolved env SecretRef snapshot.

**Evidence after fix:** Redacted terminal output from the production-path proof command:

```text
real-behavior-proof: googlechat SecretRef precedence
credential availability check: no GOOGLE_CHAT_* service-account env/profile entries were present; proof uses redacted service-account JSON through production resolver paths
mixed ref-plus-inline: FAIL_CLOSED
mixed ref-plus-inline error: channels.googlechat.accounts.default.serviceAccount: unresolved SecretRef "file:filemain:/channels/googlechat/missing-service-account". Resolve this command against an active gateway runtime snapshot before reading it.
mixed ref-plus-inline inline fallback used: false
resolved snapshot assignments: channels.googlechat.accounts.prod.serviceAccount
resolved snapshot warnings: SECRETS_REF_OVERRIDES_PLAINTEXT
resolved snapshot serviceAccountRef removed: true
resolved snapshot account source: inline
resolved snapshot client_email: REDACTED@redacted-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com
resolved snapshot private_key: REDACTED
resolved snapshot inline legacy used: false
```

**Observed result after fix:** The stale mixed `serviceAccountRef` plus inline `serviceAccount` case failed closed before inline fallback, and the resolved snapshot path produced an account with the redacted resolved service-account email, removed `serviceAccountRef`, and did not use the legacy inline email.

**What was not tested:** A live Google Chat API send/webhook with real service-account credentials was not tested because this machine did not expose Google Chat credentials. The exercised paths are the production Google Chat credential resolver and runtime SecretRef resolver, not mocked test-only helpers.

## Maintainer compatibility note
This intentionally keeps the documented fail-closed precedence: when `serviceAccountRef` is present but unresolved, Google Chat does not fall back to inline `serviceAccount`, even if the inline credentials are otherwise valid. Existing stale mixed configs must repair/remove the stale ref or resolve it successfully.

## Tests
- `pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/setup.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/secret-contract.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md extensions/googlechat/src/accounts.ts extensions/googlechat/src/secret-contract.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/secret-contract.ts extensions/googlechat/src/setup.test.ts`
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json`
- `pnpm check:changed`

target.title: feat(compaction): add identifier survival validation after summarization
target.body:
Fixes #90010
Fixes #79588

## Summary

Improve compaction identifier survival with transcript-wide extraction and quality-guard retry hints. Fixes two security/correctness issues found during review:

**Fix 1 — Sanitize string-encoded JSON arguments before extraction**: `extractMessageTextForIdentifiers` now parses string-encoded tool arguments as JSON and passes the parsed object through `sanitizeDiagnosticPayload` before identifier scanning. Previously, string arguments (e.g. OpenAI's JSON-stringified format) were appended raw, allowing credential values like `{"token":"deadbeef12345678"}` to feed the hex identifier extractor and leak into `## Exact identifiers`.

**Fix 2 — Fail strict compaction when cap recovery cannot fit**: When strict mode detects that final capping removed required identifiers and the recovery block would exceed `MAX_COMPACTION_SUMMARY_CHARS`, compaction is now cancelled instead of returning a summary with known identifier loss. This prevents the session from continuing in a state where strict-required identifiers are silently dropped.

## Changed files

- `src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard-quality.ts` — Parse string args as JSON, sanitize via `sanitizeDiagnosticPayload` before appending; non-JSON strings still pass through
- `src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts` — Cancel compaction (return `{ cancel: true }` with reason) when strict cap recovery overflows instead of silently skipping
- `src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts` — Three regression tests: string-encoded JSON credential sanitization, non-JSON passthrough, strict-mode cap overflow cancel

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Two security/correctness issues in compaction identifier survival. (1) `extractMessageTextForIdentifiers` appended string-encoded tool arguments raw, allowing credential values like `{"token":"deadbeef12345678"}` to feed the hex identifier extractor and leak into `## Exact identifiers`. (2) When strict mode detected that final capping removed required identifiers and the recovery block would exceed `MAX_COMPACTION_SUMMARY_CHARS`, the summary was returned with known identifier loss instead of cancelling compaction.

Real environment tested: macOS 15.5 arm64, Node v22.19.0, pnpm v11.2.2. Built from source on branch `compaction-identifier-survival-validation` at `/tmp/wt-75336`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

Step 1. Create proof script exercising production `extractMessageTextForIdentifiers`, `extractOpaqueIdentifiers`, `extractIdentifiersFromMessages`, `computeLostIdentifiers`, and `capCompactionSummary`:

```typescript
import {
  extractMessageTextForIdentifiers,
  extractOpaqueIdentifiers,
  extractIdentifiersFromMessages,
  computeLostIdentifiers,
} from "./src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard-quality.js";
import { testing } from "./src/agents/agent-hooks/compaction-safeguard.js";
const { capCompactionSummary, MAX_COMPACTION_SUMMARY_CHARS } = testing;
```

Step 2. Validate Fix 1, string-encoded JSON args with credential `token` field:

```typescript
const msg = {
  role: "assistant",
  content: [{
    type: "functionCall", id: "call_1", name: "configure",
    arguments: '{"token":"deadbeef12345678","endpoint":"https://api.acme.io/v2"}',
  }],
};
const text = extractMessageTextForIdentifiers(msg);
assert(!text.includes("deadbeef12345678"));    // credential redacted
assert(text.includes("https://api.acme.io/v2")); // non-credential survives
```

Step 3. Validate Fix 2, capping loses identifiers and recovery overflows:

```typescript
const transcriptIds = extractIdentifiersFromMessages([
  { role: "user", content: "deploy hash a1b2c3d4e5f6" },
]);
const oversized = "## Decisions\nKeep flow.\n## Exact identifiers\n"
  + "x".repeat(MAX_COMPACTION_SUMMARY_CHARS) + "\na1b2c3d4e5f6";
const capped = capCompactionSummary(oversized);
const lost = computeLostIdentifiers(transcriptIds, capped);
assert(lost.length > 0);  // identifier at tail truncated by capping
const recovered = `${capped}\n\n## Exact identifiers (recovered)\n- ${lost[0]}`;
assert(recovered.length > MAX_COMPACTION_SUMMARY_CHARS); // recovery overflows
```

Step 4. Run `npx tsx proof-75336.ts` from the patched branch.

Evidence after fix:

```console
$ cd /tmp/wt-75336 && npx tsx proof-75336.ts

[Proof 1] String-encoded JSON args sanitization
  PASS credential token value is redacted from string args
  PASS non-credential endpoint survives sanitization
  PASS hex identifier extractor does not capture redacted credential
  PASS non-JSON string args pass through for extraction

[Proof 2] Identifier loss detection after capping
  PASS transcript identifiers extracted from messages
  PASS hex identifier normalized to uppercase
  PASS capped summary respects size limit
  PASS identifier at tail is lost after capping
  PASS recovery block exceeds cap (triggers cancel in strict mode)

Results: 9 passed, 0 failed
```

Observed result after fix:

Fix 1: `extractMessageTextForIdentifiers` no longer leaks credential-field values from string-encoded JSON arguments. The `token` field value `deadbeef12345678` is redacted by `sanitizeDiagnosticPayload` before the hex identifier extractor can capture it. Non-credential fields and non-JSON strings pass through normally.

Fix 2: When strict mode detects identifier loss after capping and the recovery block would exceed `MAX_COMPACTION_SUMMARY_CHARS` (16,000 chars), compaction is cancelled with a diagnostic reason instead of returning a summary with known missing identifiers. The cancel reason is stored via `setCompactionSafeguardCancelReason` for downstream diagnostics.

What was not tested: Live compaction with a real model and conversation history (requires a running OpenClaw session with enough context to trigger compaction).

target.title: feat(feishu): add segment streaming mode
target.body:
## Summary

Add an opt-in Feishu streaming delivery mode that accumulates model output and flushes complete segments at pause points, without changing the existing CardKit streaming-card default.

## Motivation

Feishu currently uses `channels.feishu.streaming` for CardKit live streaming. That behavior is useful when users want one live card that updates as the model generates text.

Some workflows need a different delivery style: show completed model text before a tool call, keep tool/status output in order, and then send the follow-up answer after the model resumes. Sending these boundaries as complete segments avoids sentence-by-sentence card updates while still giving users useful progress before the final reply.

## Configuration Behavior

`streaming` remains the top-level enable/disable switch for Feishu streaming delivery. `streamingMode` only applies when `streaming` is enabled.

| `streaming` | `streamingMode` | Behavior                                                                                                                                                |
| ----------- | --------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `false`     | `"card"`        | Streaming is disabled. Feishu sends replies through the regular non-streaming path; the mode value is ignored.                                          |
| `false`     | `"segment"`     | Streaming is disabled. Partial text is not accumulated and pause-point segment flushing is not enabled; the mode value is ignored.                      |
| `true`      | `"card"`        | Existing CardKit streaming behavior. Feishu updates one live card and closes it with the final content. This remains the default.                       |
| `true`      | `"segment"`     | New pause-point segment behavior. Feishu accumulates partial text and sends complete segments on tool start, idle, block, tool payload, or final reply. |

`chunkMode` still applies after a reply or segment is ready to send. In segment mode, `streamingMode: "segment"` decides when to flush a semantic segment, then `chunkMode` decides whether that segment needs to be split by length or newline before sending to Feishu.

## Changes

- Add `channels.feishu.streamingMode` with supported values:
  - `"card"`: default, existing CardKit streaming behavior
  - `"segment"`: accumulate model text and flush at pause points
- Preserve current `channels.feishu.streaming` semantics:
  - `true` enables streaming delivery
  - `false` disables streaming delivery
  - default mode remains CardKit streaming
- In segment mode:
  - partial model output is accumulated
  - accumulated text is flushed on tool start, idle, block, tool payload, or final reply
  - duplicate final text after an idle flush is suppressed
  - segment sends are serialized so final/tool payloads cannot overtake an in-flight partial flush
  - each flushed segment independently uses `renderMode: "auto"`
  - mentions are attached only to the first text segment
  - reasoning preview callbacks stay disabled because segment mode does not use live cards
- Update Feishu config schema, generated config metadata, docs, and tests.

## Example

Use segment delivery with:

```json5
{
  channels: {
    feishu: {
      streaming: true,
      streamingMode: "segment",
    },
  },
}
```

Default behavior remains:

```json5
{
  channels: {
    feishu: {
      streaming: true,
      streamingMode: "card",
    },
  },
}
```

## Compatibility

- Existing configs that only set `channels.feishu.streaming` keep the same behavior because `streamingMode` defaults to `"card"`.
- `streaming: false` continues to disable streaming delivery regardless of `streamingMode`.
- Segment mode is opt-in and does not change CardKit streaming-card behavior.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Feishu configs can opt into `streamingMode: "segment"`, and the dispatcher sends one completed segment before a tool pause and a second completed segment after the tool returns.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw gateway connected to a private Feishu DM using the dev Feishu account, running the PR checkout `feishu-segment-streaming-mode-clean` at commit `ad678ae16e9`. Node.js `v22.22.2`; pnpm dependencies installed in `/root/.openclaw/workspace-dev/projects/fork-repos/openclaw`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Sent this Feishu DM to the dev agent: `First reply with exactly one line: segment-one-ok. Then you must call a tool to check the current date or time. After the tool returns, reply with exactly one line: segment-two-ok.` The dev Feishu account was configured with `streaming: true` and `streamingMode: "segment"`.
- Evidence after fix: Redacted runtime log and copied live output are included below. Gateway journal shows `segment flush reason=toolStart` followed by `segment flush reason=final`; agent session output shows `segment-one-ok`, `toolCall: session_status`, and `segment-two-ok`.
- Observed result after fix: The live Feishu turn emitted `segment-one-ok`, paused for the `session_status` tool call, then emitted `segment-two-ok`. Gateway logs show two segment flushes for the same Feishu turn, first with `reason=toolStart` and then with `reason=final`, and the dispatch completed with `replies=2`.
- What was not tested: Broad multi-user/load behavior and media upload fallback in a real Feishu tenant were not tested in this live run; media fallback behavior is covered by the targeted Feishu dispatcher tests below.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this patch, Feishu had no `streamingMode: "segment"` branch, so this live turn would use the existing CardKit streaming-card/default delivery path instead of two segment flushes.

Copied and redacted gateway journal plus agent-session output from the live Feishu run:

```text
# Redacted OpenClaw gateway journal, 2026-05-06 23:12 Asia/Shanghai
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot]: received message from <redacted_sender> in <redacted_chat> (p2p)
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot]: Feishu[dev-bot] DM from <redacted_sender>: First reply with exactly one line: segment-one-ok. Then you must call a tool to check the current date or time. After the tool returns, reply with exactly one line: segment-two-ok.
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot]: dispatching to agent (session=agent:dev:feishu:direct:<redacted_sender>)
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot] segment flush reason=toolStart mode=text chars=14
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot] segment flush reason=final mode=text chars=14
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot]: dispatch complete (queuedFinal=true, replies=2)

# Redacted agent session transcript for the same Feishu turn
assistant text: segment-one-ok
toolCall: session_status
toolResult: ok=true, sessionKey=agent:dev:feishu:direct:<redacted_sender>
assistant text: segment-two-ok
```

Supplemental schema/metadata proof from the same checkout:

```text
$ node --import tsx -e 'import { FeishuConfigSchema } from "./extensions/feishu/src/config-schema.ts"; import { GENERATED_BUNDLED_CHANNEL_CONFIG_METADATA } from "./src/config/bundled-channel-config-metadata.generated.ts"; const parsed = FeishuConfigSchema.parse({ streaming: true, streamingMode: "segment", accounts: { main: { streaming: true, streamingMode: "card" } } }); const feishu = GENERATED_BUNDLED_CHANNEL_CONFIG_METADATA.find((entry) => entry.channelId === "feishu"); console.log("top-level streamingMode:", parsed.streamingMode); console.log("account streamingMode:", parsed.accounts.main.streamingMode); console.log("metadata streamingMode enum:", feishu.schema.properties.streamingMode.enum.join(",")); console.log("account metadata streamingMode enum:", feishu.schema.properties.accounts.additionalProperties.properties.streamingMode.enum.join(","));'
top-level streamingMode: segment
account streamingMode: card
metadata streamingMode enum: card,segment
account metadata streamingMode enum: card,segment
```

## Tests

- `node_modules/.bin/vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.test.ts --reporter=dot` - passed, 3 test files / 102 tests
- `pnpm config:channels:check` - passed
- `git diff --check` - passed

target.title: fix(control-ui): share gateway token scope across loopback hosts
target.body:
Repair the existing #43254 branch rather than replacing it. This keeps gateway tokens in sessionStorage, shares token scope only across true same-machine loopback aliases, and preserves protocol, port, and pathname boundaries so distinct local gateways remain isolated.

This repair addresses the hydrated bot findings by avoiding broad 127.* hostname matching, removing misleading unreachable IPv6 handling, covering localhost/127/[::1] same-port sharing, and adding negative coverage for DNS hostnames and different ports.

Credit: original implementation and reproduction analysis by @ssdiwu in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/43254. #69647 is related but tracks a different reverse-proxy path persistence bug and should stay separate. Security-routed linked issues remain out of this fix path.

target.title: chore(ci): harden package-manager resolution
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: CI and repo scripts had several package-resolution escape hatches: `pnpm dlx`, docs publish `npm install --no-save --package-lock=false`, non-hash-pinned pre-commit hook refs, stale pnpm pins, and Python CI installs that only implicitly relied on repo-root uv config.
- Why it matters: These paths are package supply-chain risk multipliers. They let trusted CI jobs resolve code at runtime from mutable registries, tags, or one-off install commands, which widens the window where a compromised, newly published, or retagged package could execute before maintainers have time to review or react.
- What changed: This PR makes those fetch paths more boring and reviewable: repo scripts use lockfile-pinned dev dependencies instead of `pnpm dlx`; CI Python installs explicitly load repo uv policy; pre-commit hooks are pinned to immutable commits; stale pnpm pins are aligned; and pnpm/uv both enforce the same two-day minimum package age gate.
- Regression guardrail: Root `AGENTS.md` now records these CI/pre-commit and package-manager supply-chain rules so agentic coding does not reintroduce mutable refs or one-off installs.
- Lockfile note: The `pnpm-lock.yaml` diff is expected. Replacing `pnpm dlx` with `pnpm exec` means the tools previously fetched on demand now live as dev dependencies, so their transitive dependency graphs are reviewed and pinned in the lockfile. Most of the noise comes from `knip` pulling optional/native `oxc` packages for multiple platforms.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: These gaps came from convenience/bootstrap patterns that are common in CI: one-off `dlx` tools, ad-hoc docs installs, version-tag pre-commit hooks, and Python installs that happened to respect repo config only because they ran from the repo root.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There was no single guardrail preventing package-manager commands from resolving fresh packages outside the repo lockfile or configured age gate.
- Contributing context (if known): pnpm already had a minimum-release-age policy, but not every CI package fetch path was obviously tied to it.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `Yes`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: This PR reduces package-fetch risk by making CI installs prefer lockfile/configured package-manager paths, immutable hook refs, and uv's repo-scoped `exclude-newer = "2 days"` policy. The lockfile changes are intentional: replacing `pnpm dlx` with `pnpm exec` requires adding those tools as dev dependencies so their transitive graphs are reviewed and pinned. Most of the lockfile noise comes from `knip` pulling optional/native `oxc` packages for multiple platforms.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm 10.33.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): CI/package-manager workflows
- Relevant config (redacted): pnpm `minimumReleaseAge: 2880`; uv `exclude-newer = "2 days"`

### Steps

1. `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --ignore-scripts=false --config.engine-strict=false --config.enable-pre-post-scripts=true`
2. `pnpm build`
3. `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 .github/workflows/ci.yml pnpm-workspace.yaml pyproject.toml`
4. `pnpm check:changed`

### Expected

- Install and build pass.
- Changed-file formatting passes.
- Package-manager hardening does not introduce build failures.

### Actual

- Install passed.
- Build passed.
- Changed-file formatting passed.
- `pnpm check:changed` passed conflict-marker checks, typecheck, lint, and import-cycle checks; full tests still fail in existing gateway/auth expectation cases unrelated to these package-manager changes.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Relevant local check summary:

```text
[check:changed] summary
   1.19s  ok         conflict markers
  19.42s  ok         typecheck all
  21.29s  ok         lint
   5.75s  ok         runtime import cycles
 206.41s  failed:1   tests all
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: lockfile install, build, changed-file formatting, and changed-check non-test lanes.

target.title: Make typing indicator TTL configurable
target.body:
## Summary

- Increase the default typing indicator TTL from 2 minutes to 5 minutes.
- Add configurable `typingTtlSeconds` support via session config and agent defaults.
- Document the new setting and regenerate the base config schema.

Closes #71999

## Tests

- `pnpm config:schema:check`
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/typing-persistence.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `typingTtlSeconds` is accepted in both the real OpenClaw CLI config schema paths that this PR adds (`session.typingTtlSeconds` and `agents.defaults.typingTtlSeconds`).
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, running the built CLI from this PR head `e4c78ec1c8bc` with an isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Created a temporary `openclaw.json` containing `session.typingTtlSeconds: 45` and `agents.defaults.typingTtlSeconds: 420`, then ran `pnpm openclaw --no-color config validate`, `pnpm openclaw --no-color config get session.typingTtlSeconds`, and `pnpm openclaw --no-color config get agents.defaults.typingTtlSeconds`.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the real CLI:

  ```text
  > openclaw@2026.5.4 openclaw /Users/suntianpei/Documents/Playground/openclaw
  > node scripts/run-node.mjs --no-color config validate

  Config valid: /var/folders/.../openclaw.json

  > node scripts/run-node.mjs --no-color config get session.typingTtlSeconds
  45

  > node scripts/run-node.mjs --no-color config get agents.defaults.typingTtlSeconds
  420
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** The real CLI validated the config successfully and read back both TTL values exactly, confirming the new config keys are accepted after the patch.
- **What was not tested:** No live chat-channel typing indicator was sent; this proof covers real CLI/config acceptance, with runtime TTL precedence covered by the focused automated tests.

target.title: feat(heartbeat): support model fallbacks via {primary,fallbacks} (#69434)
target.body:
## Summary

- Accept `agents.*.heartbeat.model` as `{ primary, fallbacks }` in addition to the existing string form, mirroring `agents.defaults.model`. Closes #69434.
- Heartbeat ticks now fail over to the next fallback on retriable provider errors (rate-limit/429, overload/502/503, timeout) so primary-provider quota exhaustion no longer silently stalls the heartbeat loop for hours.
- String form stays fully backwards-compatible; new object form is additive.

## Design

- Schema: `src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts` reuses `AgentModelSchema` (the same union already used by `agents.defaults.model`, `imageModel`, etc.), and `src/config/types.agent-defaults.ts` replaces `model?: string` with `model?: AgentModelConfig`.
- Plumbing: `heartbeat-runner.ts` resolves primary + fallbacks via the shared `resolveAgentModelPrimaryValue` / `resolveAgentModelFallbackValues` helpers and passes them through a new `GetReplyOptions.heartbeatModelFallbacks` to the reply pipeline. `get-reply-run.ts` then stores them on a new `FollowupRun.run.modelFallbacksOverride` field. `resolveModelFallbackOptions` (used by both `agent-runner-execution.ts` and the memory flush path) and the inline fallback in `followup-runner.ts` prefer this override over `resolveRunModelFallbacksOverride`, so the existing `runWithModelFallback` machinery and its retriable-error classifier (429/502/503/timeout) transparently pick up the heartbeat chain.
- `get-reply.ts` switches the defaults-fallback read to `resolveAgentModelPrimaryValue(agentCfg?.heartbeat?.model)` so object-form defaults resolve correctly.
- `HeartbeatSummary` (consumed by `/status`, `/health`, compaction diagnostics, run attempts) keeps `model?: string` (now the primary) and gains an optional `modelFallbacks?: string[]` — no existing reader breaks.
- `gateway/model-pricing-cache.ts` migrates `heartbeat.model` registration from `addResolvedModelRef` to `addModelListLike`, so heartbeat fallbacks get the same pricing prefetch treatment as the agent-level model list.
- Empty `fallbacks: []` in the object form disables the fallback chain for that heartbeat without replacing it.

## Docs / config help

- `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md` documents both forms and the retriable-error failover semantics.
- `src/config/schema.help.ts` + `src/config/schema.labels.ts` add help text and labels for `heartbeat.model`, `.primary`, and `.fallbacks` at both defaults and per-agent scopes.
- Changelog entry under `## Unreleased` → `### Changes`.

## Test plan

- [x] New unit tests in `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.model-override.test.ts`:
  - passes primary + fallbacks when `heartbeat.model` is an object
  - omits fallbacks when object form has no `fallbacks` array
  - propagates per-agent heartbeat fallbacks after merging defaults
- [x] 11 pre-existing heartbeat-model-override tests still pass (string form regression guard)
- [x] `pnpm tsgo:prod` (core + extensions) green
- [x] `pnpm lint:core` 0/0
- [x] `pnpm format:check` clean
- [x] `pnpm test:changed` — 2248 tests passing across 246 files
- [x] `pnpm config:schema:gen` + `pnpm config:docs:gen` baselines regenerated and checked in
- [ ] Manual: verify a 429-exhausted primary provider actually fails over to a second provider in a live tick (covered indirectly through `runWithModelFallback` unit tests + `heartbeat-runner` tests; end-to-end live repro is in the reporter's production environment)

## Notes

- Pre-commit hook was bypassed via `FAST_COMMIT=1` (documented escape hatch) to avoid a pre-existing `src/entry.version-fast-path.test.ts:44` test-type failure on an untouched file. All other gates (format, lint, core typecheck, tests, doc baselines) pass.

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: fix(acp): treat missing cwd as stale bound session
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: a bound ACP session can stay attached after its persisted runtime working directory disappears, so retries keep failing against a dead session and the conversation stays wedged.
- Why it matters: affected conversations remain wedged until the stale binding state is manually cleared.
- What changed: during ACP dispatch, treat a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` as stale when the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` can no longer be resolved on disk (`ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR`), then unbind the dead conversation. The signal is folded into the existing `isStaleSessionInitError` classifier and the existing `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations` dispatch helper — no new module, no parallel helper.
- Scope: dispatch-only. Two files: `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts` and its test.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- ACP stale-session classifier (`isStaleSessionInitError`) extended to accept the bound session's persisted cwd.
- Auto-reply ACP dispatch stale-binding cleanup (`maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`) passes the persisted cwd at the `runTurn` catch.
- Regression tests: generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` + missing persisted cwd unbinds; generic init failure + existing cwd does not.

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #60720
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

The dispatch path already removes stale ACP bindings for selected ACP session init failures, but it classified staleness from the error message only. The live missing-cwd case surfaces as a generic spawn `ENOENT` wrapped as `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, whose message does not match the stale-message set. The reliable signal is the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd`: when it no longer resolves to a directory, the binding is unrecoverable. Without checking it, the stale bound conversation survives the failed dispatch and keeps routing future turns into the dead ACP session.

## Implementation Notes

- Rebased onto current `main` after the `@openclaw/acp-core` runtime extraction. The fix now extends `main`'s existing in-file `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`/`isStaleSessionInitError` instead of introducing a separate `src/acp/runtime/stale-session.ts` helper (which no longer fits the post-extraction layout). The earlier `extensions/memory-core` test shim is dropped — current `main` carries that mock contract natively.
- Staleness now requires **filesystem-confirmed** absence of the persisted cwd. The unbind cannot be triggered by an error-message string alone, which removes an over-removal vector (e.g. a transient tool error whose text mentions a missing working directory). `ENOENT` and `ENOTDIR` count as missing; a present-but-unreadable cwd (`EACCES`) is treated as present so a transient permission/mount issue never severs a live conversation.

## Maintainer Decision (session-state semantics)

This removes persisted bound-conversation state when a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` is paired with a filesystem-confirmed missing cwd — the recovery is correct for a permanently gone cwd, but it is session-state behavior worth explicit maintainer acceptance (per ClawSweeper's `merge-risk: session-state`). Two residual, deliberately out-of-scope behaviors to weigh:

- A transient filesystem outage (mount briefly returning `ENOENT`) on a still-valid cwd would unbind a live session. Mitigated by treating only `ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR` (not `EACCES`) as missing, but not eliminated.
- `unbind({ targetSessionKey })` removes all bindings for that session (pre-existing helper scope). For a shared-cwd session that is arguably correct, since the cwd is shared by all its bindings.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A bound ACP session whose persisted runtime `cwd` no longer exists should be treated as stale during dispatch cleanup, unbinding the affected conversation instead of keeping retries stuck on the same dead session.
- Real environment tested: Disposable local OpenClaw gateway from this PR checkout on NixOS Linux, Telegram DM via staging bot `@kesslerClawBot`, loopback gateway port `18794`, cron/heartbeats disabled. Gateway was stopped after the proof run.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the disposable staging gateway from PR head `1ad568ba1e0db9051904eacee025ee36ec2f06ee`, sent a Telegram DM to `@kesslerClawBot` that routed through a bound ACP conversation whose persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` pointed at `/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843`, let ACP dispatch fail with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, then inspected the post-run binding state.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Redacted public proof artifact: https://gist.github.com/kesslerio/dbefdac0c5d5a5849997b6bf702047c6. Follow-up proof comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/68843#issuecomment-4558022819.

  ```txt
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.491-07:00 telegram: routed via bound conversation 8352721935 -> agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.520-07:00 telegram inbound: chatId=8352721935 ... preview="[...] PR68843 patched missing cwd cleanup proof"
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.365-07:00 [plugins] embedded acpx runtime backend ready
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.400-07:00 dispatch-acp: removed 1 stale bound conversation(s) for agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 after ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED: Failed to spawn agent command: /run/current-system/sw/bin/codex-acp-openclaw
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.404-07:00 acp-dispatch: session=agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 outcome=error code=ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED latencyMs=327 queueDepth=0 activeRuntimes=0
  ```

  Post-run binding state:

  ```json
  {
    "bindingCount": 0,
    "sessionKey": "agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1",
    "acpState": "error",
    "runtimeOptions": {
      "cwd": "/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843"
    }
  }
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the staging Telegram DM routed through the stale bound ACP conversation, ACPX reached the runtime backend, dispatch failed with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, and the stale bound conversation was removed because the persisted ACP cwd was missing. Post-run binding state showed `bindingCount: 0`.
- What was not tested: Production AlphaClaw was not used. This proof uses the disposable staging gateway and staging Telegram bot to avoid disrupting the live Telegram poller. A broader channel-neutral ACP proof was not run because the Telegram staging proof exercises the dispatch cleanup path directly.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the raw local `/home/art/...` gateway log is retained only as an operator audit pointer because it contains unnecessary routing identifiers and machine-specific details. Public proof is the redacted gist and copied transcript above.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): current `main` only treated selected ACP init-failure messages as stale and did not inspect persisted ACP session `cwd` metadata for a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`; ClawSweeper confirmed the source-level pre-fix path in its review comment.

## Tests and validation

Commands run on rebased head `79c9e2392d` (worktree off current `origin/main`):

```sh
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts -t "stale|cwd"
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
```

Results: formatting passed; focused stale/cwd dispatch suite passed (5 passed, 50 skipped — including the new missing-cwd-unbinds and existing-cwd-does-not-unbind cases); core test typecheck passed (exit 0).

## Security Impact

No new permissions, secrets handling, network calls, command execution surface, or data access scope. The behavioral expansion is limited to unbinding stale ACP conversations after an ACP init failure when the persisted ACP cwd is filesystem-confirmed missing.

## Compatibility / Migration

No config or migration required. The change affects only stale ACP init failure recovery.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted rebase onto current `main`, fold-in to the existing dispatch helper, structural/correctness/adversarial review hardening, validation, and PR-body update. The live staging proof above remains valid: it unbound via the generic `Failed to spawn agent command` failure plus the missing-cwd probe, which is exactly the path this rebased head preserves.

target.title: fix(cron): prevent scheduler death when startup catch-up fails
target.body:
## Summary

When runMissedJobs() throws during start(), the timer was never armed, silently killing the cron scheduler until the next gateway restart.

## Root Cause

The start() function in src/cron/service/ops.ts calls runMissedJobs() outside of any try/catch, followed by the locked() block that calls armTimer(state). If runMissedJobs() throws (e.g. an isolated agent turn fails during startup catch-up), execution never reaches armTimer(), and the scheduler is permanently dead.

## Changes

1. **Guard armTimer in start()** - Wrap runMissedJobs() in try/catch so the timer is always armed
2. **Zombie detection in onTimer()** - Clear stale runningAtMs markers at runtime (threshold: 2x job timeout or 2 min) so jobs recover without restart
3. **Tests** - Verify timer stays armed on catch-up failure and stale markers are cleared

Fixes #67854
Related: #59056, #60799

target.title: Fix session transcript path canonicalization on save
target.body:
# OpenClaw session-store path fix, 2026-04-12

## Status
Applied live to installed runtime. Also ported carefully into a fresh source clone of the matching `v2026.4.11` upstream tag, but not yet merged upstream or installed from that source tree.

## Source clone
- Repo: `/Users/rocklobster/.openclaw/workspace/external/openclaw-upstream`
- Branch: `fix/session-store-transcript-path-canonicalization`

## Protected file
`/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/store-Cw5RrVCw.js`

## Backup
`/Users/rocklobster/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/backups/runtime-patches/store-Cw5RrVCw.js.pre-20260412-1233`

## What changed
Added a save-time canonicalization step so persisted `sessionFile` and `transcriptPath` values are rewritten into the real sessions directory before session-store writes hit disk.

## Why
Without this, restored/recovered session entries could keep workspace-style transcript paths, then later be treated as missing/orphaned and get swept or misread.

## Verification performed
- syntax check passed
- synthetic save test passed
- gateway restarted cleanly
- post-restart audit:
  - `entries 913`
  - `legacy_non_agent_keys 0`
  - `non_cron_duplicate_sessionids 0`
  - `missing_jsonl_paths 0`

## Release blocker rule
Treat this as a release blocker until the fix is confirmed upstream and in the installed version. Before any OpenClaw update or manual overwrite touching runtime/config/session storage, stop and verify this protection first.

## Source patch details
Changed files in the source clone:
- `src/config/sessions/store.ts`
- `src/config/sessions/types.ts`
- `src/config/sessions/store.pruning.integration.test.ts`

Validation in source clone:
- `node --no-maglev node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/config/sessions/sessions.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.pruning.integration.test.ts`
- Result: `2 passed`, `29 passed`

## Patch export
- Mailbox-style patch export written for handoff/cherry-pick use:
  - `/Users/rocklobster/.openclaw/workspace/patches/openclaw-session-store-path-fix-2026-04-12.patch`

## Current git state
- Branch is clean and still anchored on tag base `v2026.4.11`
- Head commit:
  - `e1c0d1a` `Fix session transcript path canonicalization on save`

## Next upstream action
Open or stage an upstream PR from the source clone using commit `e1c0d1a` or the exported patch above. Keep the live runtime bandage in place until an official release is installed and re-verified on this machine.

target.title: fix: Mattermost interaction token forgeable via hardcoded HMAC...
target.body:
## Fix Summary
The `deriveInteractionSecret` function in the Mattermost extension uses a hardcoded public string as the HMAC key, making the interaction signing secret fully reproducible by anyone who knows the bot token. An attacker who obtains the bot token can forge valid `_token` values, craft arbitrary button-click POST requests to the gateway's interaction endpoint, and trigger agent system events (`core.system.enqueueSystemEvent`) as if a legitimate user clicked an approved Mattermost button.

## Issue Linkage
Fixes #64545

## Security Snapshot
- CVSS v3.1: 7.1 (High)
- CVSS v4.0: 7.1 (High)

## Implementation Details
### Files Changed
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema-core.ts` (+1/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema.test.ts` (+19/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.test.ts` (+23/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.ts` (+8/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.test.ts` (+20/-17)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts` (+23/-10)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` (+2/-3)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/send.ts` (+8/-2)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/types.ts` (+2/-0)

### Technical Analysis
1. Obtain the Mattermost bot token (available via the Mattermost admin panel, gateway environment variables, or any configuration leak).
2. Reproduce the derivation: `derivedSecret = HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` — this is deterministic and requires no further gateway access.
3. Construct an action context matching an existing interactive post, e.g., `{"action_id": "approve", "post_id": "<target_post_id>", "channel_id": "<target_channel_id>"}`.
4. Compute the forged token: `_token = HMAC-SHA256(derivedSecret, JSON.stringify(sortedContext))`.
5. POST a crafted payload to `http://<gateway-host>:<gatewayPort>/mattermost/interactions/<accountId>` with the forged `_token` field.
6. `verifyInteractionToken` at `interactions.ts:165-175` passes; the handler dispatches `core.system.enqueueSystemEvent` to the agent as if a legitimate user clicked an approved button.

## Validation Evidence
- Command: `pnpm exec oxlint src/ && pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`
- Status: passed (with pre-existing baseline failures)

## Risk and Compatibility
- non-breaking; no known regression impact

## AI-Assisted Disclosure
- AI-assisted: yes
- Model: github-copilot/claude-sonnet-4.6

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Mattermost interaction tokens should no longer be forgeable from knowledge of the bot token; the branch now uses a generated or configured interaction secret instead of the old deterministic `HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` derivation.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on the rebased PR head `e261bcfab6de7ff05d18d2493ea60aedf234498d`, running the real Mattermost interaction module through Node with `tsx`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --input-type=module -e 'import { createHmac } from "node:crypto"; import { ensureInteractionSecret, generateInteractionToken, verifyInteractionToken } from "./extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts"; const botToken = "redacted-bot-token-for-proof"; const oldDerivedSecret = createHmac("sha256", "openclaw-mattermost-interactions").update(botToken).digest("hex"); const generatedSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("proof-account"); const configuredSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("configured-account", "configured-proof-secret"); const context = { action_id: "approve", proof: "after-fix" }; const validToken = generateInteractionToken(context, "proof-account"); const oldForgedToken = createHmac("sha256", oldDerivedSecret).update(JSON.stringify(context)).digest("hex"); console.log(JSON.stringify({ generatedSecretLength: generatedSecret.length, generatedSecretIsHex: /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/.test(generatedSecret), generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation: generatedSecret === oldDerivedSecret, configuredSecretPreserved: configuredSecret === "configured-proof-secret", generatedTokenVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, validToken, "proof-account"), oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, oldForgedToken, "proof-account") }, null, 2));'
```

- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "generatedSecretLength": 64,
  "generatedSecretIsHex": true,
  "generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation": false,
  "configuredSecretPreserved": true,
  "generatedTokenVerifies": true,
  "oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies": false
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The generated interaction secret is random-looking 64-character hex and does not equal the old bot-token-derived secret. A token generated with the new per-account secret verifies, while a token forged from the old bot-token-derived secret does not verify.
- What was not tested: Full live Mattermost server button-click delivery was not exercised in this local proof; focused local runtime proof and Mattermost extension tests were run for the changed interaction-secret behavior.

target.title: fix(agent): surface non_deliverable_terminal_turn with specific message and auto-retry
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When a model turn ends with `stop_reason: tool_use` but no explicit terminal delivery, `resolveAttemptTrajectoryTerminal` classifies it as `non_deliverable_terminal_turn`. Previously, this was only recorded in trajectory metadata. The embedded runner's retry loop didn't handle it, and the reply layer treated it as a generic failure with misleading "/new" remediation.

## Why This Change Was Made

Three-layer fix, each touching only what it owns:

1. **run.ts (embedded runner)** — adds a single-retry for `non_deliverable_terminal_turn` in the existing retry loop, following the same pattern as `emptyResponseRetryAttempts` / `reasoningOnlyRetryAttempts`. On exhaustion, sets `meta.error.kind = "non_deliverable_terminal_turn"` via the existing `incompleteTurnErrorKind` variable.

2. **types.ts** — adds `"non_deliverable_terminal_turn"` to the `meta.error.kind` union. No other type surfaces changed. `trajectoryTerminalError` stays on `EmbeddedRunAttemptResult` (internal only), never exposed on `EmbeddedAgentRunMeta`.

3. **agent-runner-execution.ts** — detects `meta.error?.kind === "non_deliverable_terminal_turn"` with no payload text, and surfaces the specific actionable user message instead of generic fallback.

## User Impact

- Intermittent `non_deliverable_terminal_turn` errors self-recover via auto-retry at the embedded runner level (1 retry — model produced no output, retry is idempotent)
- When retry fails, user sees a specific message instead of generic "Something went wrong… use /new"
- `/new` is no longer the primary remediation for this class of failure

## Files changed

- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` — +1 line: `trajectoryTerminalError` in return value
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/types.ts` — +2 lines: type field (internal)
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/types.ts` — +1 line: `"non_deliverable_terminal_turn"` in error kind union
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts` — +26 lines: retry counter, retry block, error kind variable, log update
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-failure-copy.ts` — +6 lines: user-facing message constant
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts` — +23 lines: specific message handler
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts` — +64 lines: 2 new tests

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `non_deliverable_terminal_turn` surfaced as generic "Something went wrong… use /new" with no diagnostic signal and no retry.

Real environment tested: Linux x86_64, Node v22.19.0, OpenClaw branch fix/non-deliverable-terminal-turn-message.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `git diff HEAD~1..HEAD --stat` — 7 files, +120/-3, single commit
2. `pnpm build` — clean build, exit 0
3. `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution` — 208/208 passed (+2 new tests)
4. `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt-trajectory-status` — 19/19 passed
5. `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/result-fallback-classifier` — passed
6. `pnpm test src/trajectory/export` — passed
7. Combined: 268 tests across 4 test files, all passed

Evidence after fix:
- Retry logic in run.ts follows existing patterns (`nonDeliverableTurnRetryAttempts` counter, `continue` in the while-true loop, log.warn with provider/model context)
- `incompleteTurnErrorKind` variable selects `"non_deliverable_terminal_turn"` or `"incomplete_turn"` based on whether the retry was attempted — used in both incomplete turn return branches
- Reply layer checks `meta.error?.kind === "non_deliverable_terminal_turn"` — standard error kind dispatch, no new type fields on public meta
- 2 new tests verify: specific message surfaces when kind matches with no payload text (both empty and error-only payloads)

Observed result after fix:
- 208 agent-runner-execution tests pass (206 existing + 2 new), zero regressions
- All related test suites pass (268 total)
- Type checker passes on changed files
- Build succeeds

What was not tested: Full end-to-end reproduction with Telegram DM under heavy cacheRead load. The retry logic is at the embedded runner level and unit-testable through mock integration; live proof requires provider load to trigger the race condition.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code]

target.title: CI: report circular dependencies in PRs
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: We did not have a repo-standard way to detect and inspect runtime circular imports across `src`, `extensions`, and `scripts`.
- Why it matters: These cycles can drag large strongly connected components into the runtime graph and make lazy/runtime boundaries harder to preserve.
- What changed: I added `dependency-cruiser` repo tooling, a focused circular-dependency config, a local report script, and a non-blocking CI job that publishes PR summaries plus raw artifacts.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): I did not start failing CI on existing cycles and I did not refactor any of the current cycle clusters in this PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: N/A
- Scenario the test should lock in: N/A
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: This is tooling/CI wiring, so the direct guardrail is running the report script and validating the emitted artifacts/summary locally.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: The value here is report generation rather than product behavior. I verified the report output directly.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

PRs now get a non-blocking `dependency-circular-report` job that publishes:

- a summary table of cycle-heavy scopes
- a sorted descending directory/module list
- a Mermaid visualization for scope distribution and largest SCC clusters
- uploaded artifacts with raw JSON and full cycle listings

Local development also gets:

- `pnpm deps:circular`
- `pnpm deps:circular:json`
- `pnpm deps:circular:report`

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR] -> [standard CI lanes only] -> [no circular-import visibility]

After:
[PR] -> [dependency-circular-report job] -> [step summary + artifacts]
     -> [sorted scopes/directories + SCC graphs]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: CI now runs `dependency-cruiser` against repo sources to generate reports. This is limited to checked-in code in the Actions workspace and does not expand runtime privileges beyond normal repo CI tooling.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node.js v25.8.1 local shell
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): default repo config

### Steps

1. Run `npm exec pnpm -- run deps:circular`
2. Run `npm exec pnpm -- run deps:circular:report`
3. Inspect `.artifacts/dependency-cruiser/step-summary.md`, `report.md`, `summary.json`, and `cycle-violations.txt`

### Expected

- `dependency-cruiser` finds current runtime cycles using the repo config
- the report script emits a readable sorted summary plus raw artifacts
- CI can reuse the same script to publish a non-blocking PR report

### Actual

- Local runs completed and emitted the expected artifact set
- Current snapshot found `150` cycle violations, `24` cyclic SCCs, and `195` modules participating in cycles
- Top cycle-heavy scopes were `extensions/browser`, `src/agents`, `extensions/mattermost`, `extensions/zalouser`, and `src/cli`

## Evidence
