target.title: docs(memory): add memory system deep dive guide
target.body:
## Summary

Add a comprehensive memory system deep dive guide, addressing #43032.

### Problem

`docs/concepts/memory.md` covers basics (~600 words) but lacks depth on memory best practices, search optimization, lifecycle management, and advanced patterns.

### New document: `docs/concepts/memory-deep-dive.md` (401 lines)

Covers all 6 sections requested in the issue:

1. **Memory Architecture** — Two-layer model (`MEMORY.md` + `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`), indexing scope, what is searchable vs not
2. **Writing Effective Memory** — What belongs in MEMORY.md vs daily logs, promotion workflow, formatting tips for better search indexing
3. **Search Optimization** — Hybrid retrieval pipeline (keyword + vector + MMR), query strategies (semantic vs exact), `memory_search` → `memory_get` workflow, tuning parameters (`maxResults`, `minScore`)
4. **Memory Maintenance** — Heartbeat-based periodic review, cleanup strategies, `heartbeat-state.json` tracking
5. **Advanced Patterns** — Entity tracking with stable headings, opinion evolution with timestamps, cross-session recall via `sessionMemory`
6. **Troubleshooting** — No results, keyword-only matches, `memory_get` file restrictions, MEMORY.md bloat, missing transcript search

### Navigation

Added to `docs.json` under Sessions and memory → Memory group, right after `concepts/memory`.

### Source references

- `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/backend-config.ts` — default settings (maxResults: 6, maxSnippetChars: 700)
- `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/read-file.ts` — `memory_get` file boundary enforcement
- `src/agents/memory-search.ts` — search config resolution and sources
- `docs/concepts/memory-builtin.md` — existing builtin engine docs

Closes #43032

target.title: [codex] prefer terminal reply fields in CLI JSONL parser
target.body:
﻿﻿## Summary

- Problem: the CLI JSONL parser only special-cases Claude `type:"result"` events and otherwise falls back to generic text collection, so OpenAI/Codex-style fallback runs can lose terminal reply semantics.
- Why it matters: in channel-visible fallback paths, commentary/intermediate text can be surfaced instead of the actual final answer even when the JSONL stream already contains `phase:"final_answer"` and `task_complete.last_agent_message`.
- What changed: `parseCliJsonl()` now prefers `task_complete.last_agent_message`, then assistant messages tagged `phase:"final_answer"`, and skips `commentary`-phase generic item text.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Claude CLI handling is unchanged, no dispatch/channel code changed, and no provider failover logic changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #59918
- Related #25592
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: `parseCliJsonl()` was written around Claude `type:"result"` lines plus a generic `parsed.item.text` fallback, but it did not understand newer OpenAI/Codex-style JSONL terminal fields.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no parser preference for `task_complete.last_agent_message` or `phase:"final_answer"`, and no guard against treating `commentary` item text as the outbound reply.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the current behavior is discussed in #59918 as a narrower parser-path issue adjacent to the broader channel-leak family in #25592.
- Why this regressed now: unknown as a specific regression point; this appears more like a compatibility gap exposed by fallback runs that emit richer JSONL event shapes.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: this fix does not depend on channel dispatching, failover selection, or Claude stream-json parsing.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when JSONL contains commentary, `final_answer`, and `task_complete`, the parser returns the terminal answer; when only `final_answer` is present, it wins over commentary text.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives entirely inside the JSONL parsing function and can be reproduced with synthetic line fixtures without needing a live provider or channel.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing Claude result tests in `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts` cover the Claude-specific path and remain unchanged.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- OpenAI/Codex-style CLI fallback runs now prefer terminal reply fields instead of surfacing commentary when both are present in JSONL.
- Commentary-only generic item text is no longer treated as the final reply in this parser path.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
CLI JSONL -> generic text collection -> commentary can win -> user sees wrong reply

After:
CLI JSONL -> task_complete / final_answer preferred -> terminal answer wins -> user sees final reply
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (local contributor checkout)
- Runtime/container: Node.js + pnpm
- Model/provider: parser coverage for OpenAI/Codex-style CLI JSONL output; Claude path rechecked via existing tests
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A for unit verification
- Relevant config (redacted): CLI backend `output: "jsonl"`

### Steps

1. Feed `parseCliJsonl()` a JSONL stream containing commentary events plus a terminal `task_complete.last_agent_message`.
2. Feed `parseCliJsonl()` a JSONL stream containing commentary plus assistant `phase:"final_answer"` content.
3. Run the targeted parser tests.

### Expected

- Terminal answer fields win over commentary.
- Claude JSONL result handling still passes unchanged.

### Actual

- Verified with targeted tests after the patch.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/cli-output.test.ts src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts`
- Edge cases checked: existing Claude stream-json tests still pass; `task_complete` outranks `final_answer`; `final_answer` outranks commentary; commentary `item.text` is ignored.
- What you did **not** verify: I did not run a live WhatsApp/Telegram fallback end-to-end against a real CLI provider from this branch.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: OpenAI/Codex-style CLI JSONL output is now parsed so terminal reply records win over commentary or intermediate message text in both the full-buffer parser and streaming parser.

Real environment tested: Windows/PowerShell checkout at PR head `8b90301ef3`; installed OpenAI Codex CLI `codex-cli 0.130.0-alpha.5`; Node `v24.13.1`; OpenClaw parser entry points imported directly from `src/agents/cli-output.ts`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
codex exec --json --ephemeral --ignore-rules --sandbox read-only --skip-git-repo-check "Reply exactly: FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK" 1> .artifacts/real-proof-59920/codex-ignore-rules.jsonl 2> .artifacts/real-proof-59920/codex-ignore-rules.stderr.txt

node --import tsx --input-type=module
imports parseCliJsonl() and createCliJsonlStreamingParser() from ./src/agents/cli-output.ts
feeds the saved Codex CLI JSONL transcript through both parser paths

node --import tsx --input-type=module
imports the same parser entry points and replays redacted Codex/OpenAI terminal-field JSONL containing commentary, final_answer, and task_complete records
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ codex exec --json --ephemeral --ignore-rules --sandbox read-only --skip-git-repo-check "Reply exactly: FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK"
{"type":"thread.started","thread_id":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95"}
{"type":"turn.started"}
{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_0","type":"agent_message","text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK"}}
{"type":"turn.completed","usage":{"input_tokens":22858,"cached_input_tokens":2432,"output_tokens":129,"reasoning_output_tokens":116}}

real_codex_full_buffer_output=
{"text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK","sessionId":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95","usage":{"input":22858,"output":129,"cacheRead":2432}}
real_codex_streaming_output=
{"text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK","sessionId":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95","usage":{"input":22858,"output":129,"cacheRead":2432}}
```

```text
terminal_field_jsonl=
{"type":"response_start","thread_id":"thread-real-proof-redacted"}
{"item":{"type":"message","phase":"commentary","text":"COMMENTARY_SHOULD_NOT_WIN"}}
{"type":"response_item","payload":{"type":"message","role":"assistant","phase":"final_answer","content":[{"type":"output_text","text":"FINAL_ANSWER_SHOULD_BE_OVERRIDDEN"}]}}
{"type":"event_msg","payload":{"type":"task_complete","last_agent_message":"TASK_COMPLETE_WINS"}}
full_buffer_output=
{"text":"TASK_COMPLETE_WINS","sessionId":"thread-real-proof-redacted"}
streaming_output=
{"text":"TASK_COMPLETE_WINS","sessionId":"thread-real-proof-redacted"}
```

Observed result after fix: The installed Codex CLI produced JSONL on this machine and both OpenClaw parser paths extracted the final agent message from that transcript. For the bot-cited terminal-field shape, both parser paths returned `TASK_COMPLETE_WINS`; `COMMENTARY_SHOULD_NOT_WIN` and the lower-priority `FINAL_ANSWER_SHOULD_BE_OVERRIDDEN` were not surfaced.

What was not tested: A live WhatsApp/Telegram delivery route was not exercised for this parser-only PR.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a non-Claude JSONL backend could intentionally rely on commentary-phase `item.text` as the only final output.
  - Mitigation: the parser now only skips explicit `commentary` phase text and prefers explicit terminal fields; generic non-commentary `item.text` fallback remains intact.

target.title: fix(agents): handle explicit empty tool lists in system prompt
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2-5 bullets:

- Problem: when tools are explicitly disabled for a session, OpenClaw can still build a prompt that looks partly like a normal tool-enabled session. That can leave behind leaked skills content or other tool-only guidance.
- Why it matters: if the prompt says the session has tools when it actually does not, the model can try to use tools that are unavailable and behave like a tool-enabled session instead of a chat-only one.
- What changed: this PR makes the no-tools case consistent in the code that builds and adjusts the system prompt. The final prompt, prompt report, CLI path, embedded-runner path, and OpenAI-specific no-tools wording now all agree when a session really has no tools. It also fixes skills reporting in cases where the prompt contains another <available_skills> block or a skill body contains headings like # Project Context. Before this change, the prompt report could read the wrong block or stop too early, which could make it show missing or partial skills even though the real prompt still contained them.
- Additional behavior corrected: sessions that only have hosted/client tools no longer advertise OpenClaw built-in tool flows such as exec or subagents when those built-in tools are not actually available.
- Small contract update: provider-specific prompt code now receives `toolNames` in the `transformSystemPrompt` context, so plugins see the same advertised tool list that the main prompt builder is using.
- Scope boundary: this PR does not broadly redesign all capability handling. Sessions with omitted tool lists or non-empty filtered tool lists are still left mostly unchanged unless a narrow fix was needed to keep the no-tools and hosted-tool-only cases truthful.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related #25569, #53000, #46214
- Adjacent context: #44229, #44484, #47583
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: when OpenClaw builds the system prompt for a session with no tools, different parts of the prompt-building flow can disagree about whether tools are available. Some parts correctly treat the session as tool-free, while others still act as if tools or skills exist, so the final prompt can end up mixing no-tool behavior with tool-related guidance.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no coverage that locked in this behavior across the full flow, including the prompt text, prompt report, CLI path, embedded-runner path, provider-specific prompt code, and OpenAI-specific no-tools wording.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): #25569 is still the closest related upstream work because it distinguishes explicitly empty tool availability from unspecified/default availability, but other code paths still had inconsistent behavior. #53000 addresses similar empty-tools confusion but still keeps a reference tool catalog in the prompt. #46214 removes adjacent CLI-runner prompt pollution rather than this exact bug.
- Why this reproduces: the no-tools state was not carried through every step that contributes to the final prompt. Once a real runtime session had tools intentionally disabled, some later steps still added skills content or other tool-related guidance.
- What was ruled out: this is not just a unit-test-only mismatch. The same problem was reproduced through the normal OpenClaw CLI against an isolated deployment config, and review also surfaced related inconsistencies in provider-specific prompt code and OpenAI no-tools wording.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt-report.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt.memory.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/system-prompt.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/tool-name-allowlist.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compaction-runtime-context.test.ts`
  - `extensions/openai/index.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - explicit empty-tool sessions must not leak the skills catalog
  - explicit empty-tool sessions must not leak later tool-only guidance such as tool-call style, subagent/tool messaging guidance, approvals, or sandbox guidance
  - explicit empty-tool sessions must preserve truthful non-tool context such as workspace notes and TTS hints
  - prompt reports for explicit empty-tool sessions must keep `tools.entries = []` and no skills block
  - omitted `toolNames` must preserve legacy/default prompt behavior
  - explicit non-empty filtered tool lists must stay close to upstream/main behavior
  - client-tool-only sessions must not advertise native OpenClaw exec/subagent flows
  - provider overlays and transform hooks must receive the same effective tool context as the final prompt
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the final bug surface spans prompt construction, prompt reporting, runner plumbing, and provider overlays, so the reliable guardrail has to cover those seams rather than only the original builder helper.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none previously separated omitted tools from explicit empty effective tools across the whole prompt pipeline, and none previously locked in the client-tool-only/provider-transform cases.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Sessions with tools explicitly disabled now behave like true no-tool sessions:

- no leaked skills catalog
- no later tool-only guidance such as tool-call style, approval flow, subagent orchestration, tool-only messaging, or sandbox execution hints
- prompt report for the explicit no-tools path no longer includes a skills block
- OpenAI no-tools wording now stays aligned with the no-tools path instead of reintroducing tool-related wording
- workspace notes and TTS hints can still appear when supplied because they are context, not tool availability

Additional fixes covered by this branch:

- sessions that only have hosted/client tools no longer advertise OpenClaw built-in exec/subagent flows they do not actually have
- CLI and bundled-tool sessions continue to advertise truthful tool guidance when tools really are available
- provider-specific prompt code now receives the tool-name context it needs to make consistent prompt adjustments

Sessions with omitted tool lists and explicit non-empty filtered tool lists are still left close to existing behavior unless a narrow consistency fix required otherwise.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[toolNames omitted or []] -> [shared prompt construction] -> [explicit no-tools sessions can still inherit skills content or tool-related guidance]

After:
[toolNames omitted] -> [legacy/default prompt behavior]
[toolNames non-empty] -> [upstream/main-style filtered prompt behavior]
[toolNames = []] -> [explicit no-tools branch] -> [truthful no-tool prompt with no leaked skills block or tool-only guidance]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (Debian bookworm host)
- Runtime/container: local source checkout run via pnpm; not a containerized repro
- Model/provider: `openai-codex/gpt-5.4`
- Integration/channel (if any): CLI via `openclaw agent --local`
- Relevant config (redacted): temporary `no-tools` agent with `tools: { deny: ["*"] }`

### Steps

1. Create a disposable config copy and add a temporary no-tool agent so the runtime has a truly empty effective tool set:

   ```bash
   export OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/path/to/openclaw.json
   export OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/path/to/state-dir

   cp "$OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH" /tmp/openclaw-no-tools-repro.json
   export OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-no-tools-repro.json

   python3 - <<'PY'
   import json, os
   p = os.environ["OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH"]
   with open(p) as f:
       cfg = json.load(f)
   agents = cfg.setdefault("agents", {}).setdefault("list", [])
   agents = [a for a in agents if a.get("id") != "no-tools"]
   agents.append({
       "id": "no-tools",
       "tools": {"deny": ["*"]},
   })
   cfg["agents"]["list"] = agents
   with open(p, "w") as f:
       json.dump(cfg, f, indent=2)
       f.write("\n")
   PY
   ```

2. Run the normal CLI repro against that config:

   ```bash
   openclaw agent --local \
     --agent no-tools \
     --session-id explicit-empty-tools-repro \
     --json \
     --message '/context detail' \
     --timeout 120 \
   | jq '{payloads, tools: .meta.systemPromptReport.tools, skills: .meta.systemPromptReport.skills, systemPrompt: .meta.systemPromptReport.systemPrompt}'
   ```

### Expected

- In a real explicit no-tool session:
  - `tools.entries` should be `[]`
  - `tools.listChars` should be `0`
  - the prompt should not inject the skills catalog
  - the prompt should explicitly behave like a no-tools session

### Actual

- Live deployed-instance repro on this fixed branch:
  - `tools.entries = []`
  - `tools.listChars = 0`
  - `skills.promptChars = 0`
  - `skills.entries = []`
  - `systemPrompt.chars = 20194`
  - final assistant text includes `No tools are available in this session.`
- Live deployed-instance repro on v2026.04.11:
  - `tools.entries = []`
  - `tools.listChars = 0`
  - `skills.promptChars = 5932`
  - `skills.entries.length = 11`
  - `systemPrompt.chars = 30389`

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Evidence highlights:
- Live repro on this branch:
  - `tools.entries = []`
  - `tools.listChars = 0`
  - `skills.promptChars = 0`
  - `skills.entries = []`
  - `systemPrompt.chars = 20194`
  - assistant text explicitly says `No tools are available in this session.`
- Live repro on `v2026.4.11`:
  - `tools.entries = []`
  - `tools.listChars = 0`
  - `skills.promptChars = 5932`
  - `skills.entries.length = 11`
  - `systemPrompt.chars = 30389`
- Targeted prompt/report tests on this branch:
  - `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt-report.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt.memory.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts`

  - `extensions/openai/index.test.ts`
  - result: `150` tests passed across `8` files

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - reran the deployed-instance repro on an isolated OpenClaw instance using a disposable no-tools config
  - confirmed the bug still reproduces on `main` and `v2026.4.11`
  - confirmed the same repro no longer fails on this fixed branch

  - explicit empty-tool session keeps `tools.entries = []` and `tools.listChars = 0`

- What you did **not** verify:

- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: callers that accidentally relied on tool-related guidance surviving in explicit empty-tool sessions will now get a true no-tool prompt.

- Risk: future prompt edits could blur the difference between omitted, explicit empty, and explicit non-empty filtered tool states.

target.title: feat(doctor): add Node.js runtime info health contribution
target.body:
## Summary
Add a new Doctor health contribution that displays Node.js runtime
diagnostics during `openclaw doctor`, providing essential context for
troubleshooting and proactive upgrade guidance.
## What it does
**Runtime summary note** (always shown):
Node 24.14.0 · ~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node · via nvm
**Version manager detection** — identifies nvm, fnm, volta, asdf, n,
nodenv, nodebrew, and nvs from the executable path.
**Proactive warnings** (when applicable):
- Node version below minimum (>=22.19.0) — rarely triggers since the
  startup gate catches this first, but guards edge cases
- Node release in maintenance phase — shows months remaining until EOL
- Node release past end-of-life — recommends a current Active LTS release
- Older-than-recommended LTS — gentle nudge toward the recommended LTS
## Why
Doctor currently has no visibility into the Node.js runtime environment.
When users share Doctor output in issues or with maintainers, the Node
version and installation method are critical diagnostic context that was
previously missing.
This reuses existing infrastructure:
- `runtime-guard.ts` — `detectRuntime()`, `parseSemver()`, `runtimeSatisfies()`
- `runtime-paths.ts` — `isVersionManagedNodePath()`
No new dependencies. No runtime behavior changes outside of Doctor.
## Changes
New files:
- `src/commands/doctor-node-runtime.ts` (~200 lines) — diagnostics
  collection, warning generation, summary formatting, contribution entry
- `src/commands/doctor-node-runtime.test.ts` — 27 unit tests
Modified:
- `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts` — +1 import, +1 wrapper
  function, +1 contribution registration (after `bundled-plugin-runtime-deps`,
  before `state-integrity`)
## Test plan
- `pnpm check` — 0 errors ✅
- `vitest` — 27/27 tests ✅
- `pnpm build` — clean ✅
Test coverage:
- `detectVersionManagerName`: 11 tests (null, system, nvm, fnm, volta,
  asdf, n, nodenv, nodebrew, nvs, Windows backslash)
- `collectNodeRuntimeDiagnostics`: 3 tests (nvm path, unknown runtime,
  system install)
- `buildNodeRuntimeWarnings`: 8 tests (recommended version, below
  minimum, past EOL, maintenance phase, pre-maintenance nudge, current
  recommended, null major, future major)
- `buildNodeRuntimeSummary`: 5 tests (system install, named manager,
  unknown manager, null version, separator format)
---
## Real behavior proof
_Refreshed for current head `f90eee0a` (after the Windows case-insensitive OS-home redaction commit)._

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** The Doctor Node runtime note renders the executable path via a two-pass redaction (`shortenHomePath` for the OpenClaw home, then an OS-home pass). The OS-home pass compared the Windows home prefix with exact casing, so a valid Windows path whose drive/username casing differed from `os.homedir()` (e.g. `os.homedir()` = `C:\Users\Alice`, `execPath` = `c:\users\alice\...`) fell through unredacted and leaked the OS username. POSIX comparison stays case-sensitive; the Windows backslash-boundary prefix is now compared case-insensitively, slicing by the original length to preserve real casing.

- **Real environment tested:** Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js 24.14.0, current PR head `f90eee0a`. Invoked the real `collectNodeRuntimeDiagnostics()` + `buildNodeRuntimeSummary()` — the same render `noteNodeRuntime()` emits in the Doctor health flow — plus injected lifecycle scenarios via the exported helpers.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ran the registered render path via tsx against the real runtime:
```bash
$ node --import tsx run-node-runtime-note.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix (current head):** Block 1 is the real render on this machine. Block 2 is the POSIX OS-home redaction scenario (OPENCLAW_HOME elsewhere). Block 3 is the new Windows case-insensitive redaction this commit fixes. Remaining blocks are injected lifecycle scenarios against the current date.
```
=== 1. Real current-head render (Node v24.14.0) ===
Node 24.14.0 · /usr/bin/node · system install

=== 2. POSIX OS-home redaction (OPENCLAW_HOME=/srv/openclaw, Node under the user's OS home) ===
os home (HOME)     : /home/<user>
input  execPath    : /home/<user>/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node
output rendered    : Node 24.14.0 · ~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node · via nvm
leaks OS username? : NO (redacted)

=== 3. Windows case-insensitive redaction (the new fix) ===
os.homedir()       : C:\Users\Alice   (canonical casing)
input  execPath    : c:\users\alice\AppData\Roaming\nvm\v24.14.0\node.exe   (lower-cased)
output rendered    : Node 24.14.0 · ~\AppData\Roaming\nvm\v24.14.0\node.exe · via nvm
leaks OS username? : NO (redacted)

=== Injected Node 22 LTS (22.19.0, in maintenance) ===
Node 22 LTS is in maintenance mode (EOL 2027-04-30, ~10 months remaining).
Consider upgrading to Node 24 for the latest features and longer support.

=== Injected Node 20 (20.18.0, below the >=22.19.0 minimum) ===
Node 20.18.0 does not meet the minimum requirement (>=22.19.0).
Upgrade Node: https://nodejs.org/en/download
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The real Node 24.14.0 runtime renders a clean note (supported, system install). Both the POSIX OS-home scenario (block 2) and the Windows case-mismatch scenario (block 3) now redact the home to `~`, so no OS username appears — including when `execPath` casing differs from `os.homedir()`. Before this commit, the exact-case Windows prefix check left such paths unredacted. Injected Node 22 LTS / 20 produce the expected maintenance / below-minimum advisories. POSIX, Windows OPENCLAW_HOME-override, and the new Windows case-mismatch regression tests cover the redaction paths; 51 unit tests pass, types and lint clean.

- **What was not tested:** The full interactive `openclaw doctor` TUI run was not captured (it triggers a heavy dist build); the registered render path is invoked directly instead. Redaction relies on `os.homedir()`; in the rare case where `HOME`/`USERPROFILE` is unset and differs from the path's owner (e.g. some sudo/container setups), redaction may not apply — out of scope for this detect-only diagnostic.

target.title: feat: expose rawBody on user messages in plugin hook events
target.body:
### Summary

Add `rawBody` field to user messages in `agent_end`, `before_prompt_build`, and `before_agent_start` (legacy) hook events. This provides plugins with the original user input before channel structural context, `buildInboundUserContextPrefix()` metadata, and prompt-build hook injections are prepended.

### Motivation

Plugin hooks receive `event.messages` where user message `content` includes OpenClaw-injected metadata (timestamps, channel info, sender IDs) and other hook injections (e.g., memory context). Plugins that process user content for purposes like memory extraction, summarization, or analytics must parse and strip this metadata — a fragile approach since the format varies by channel, chat type, and OpenClaw version.

PR #51502 already established the precedent of using `RawBody` instead of `Body` to avoid passing envelope metadata to LLM processing. This PR extends that principle to plugin hook events.

### Changes

- Compute `rawBodyForHooks` from `CommandBody ?? RawBody ?? Body` in the channel message handler — the clean user text without @mentions, sender labels, or message annotations
- Thread `rawBody` through the run params chain: `get-reply-run.ts` → `runReplyAgent` → `runAgentTurnWithFallback` → `runEmbeddedPiAgent` → `runEmbeddedAttempt`
- Include `rawBody` in hook event objects for `before_prompt_build`, `before_agent_start`, and `agent_end`
- Add `rawBody` to `FollowupRun` type so the value survives queueing/deferred runs
- `rawBody` is `undefined` for non-channel runs (CLI, heartbeat, cron)
- **Steer race fix** (`86ebda8b`): Thread `rawBody` through the steer queue path so steered injections on non-Telegram channels refresh the active embedded run's hook context

### Backward Compatibility

- Additive optional field only — no breaking changes
- Plugins can feature-detect `rawBody` and fall back to `content`:
  ```typescript
  const userText = event.rawBody ?? extractText(lastUser.content);
  ```
- Plugin SDK API baseline unchanged (no drift)

### Use Case

Memory plugins send user+assistant pairs to backend for memory extraction. Without `rawBody`, the plugin must regex-strip injected XML blocks and cannot remove inbound metadata, causing noisy "memories" from message IDs and timestamps. With `rawBody`, the plugin gets clean user input directly.

### Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Plugin hook events (`before_prompt_build`, `agent_end`) now expose `event.rawBody` containing clean user-input text (22 chars) instead of requiring plugins to parse the metadata-decorated `messages[-1].content` (411 chars). Non-channel origins (heartbeat, cron, exec-event) correctly emit `rawBody: undefined`. Steered injections on non-Telegram channels now refresh `rawBody` via the steer queue path.

Real environment tested: Linux 6.17.0-1013-aws (arm64), Node v22.22.2, OpenClaw 2026.5.20, Bedrock `global.anthropic.claude-opus-4-7` (us-east-1, IAM role auth), Telegram bot `@oc_jmu_bot` via long-poll, custom probe plugin (`rawbody-probe`) using `definePluginEntry` + `api.on("before_prompt_build")` + `api.on("agent_end")`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Built from PR HEAD (`git rev-parse HEAD` = `29e89cfb84`), ran `pnpm build`, started gateway with `node openclaw.mjs gateway --allow-unconfigured --port 18799 --bind loopback --auth none --verbose`. Sent Telegram DM "hello rawbody test 004" to `@oc_jmu_bot`. Triggered heartbeat via `node openclaw.mjs system event --text "..." --mode now --url ws://127.0.0.1:18799 --token anonymous --json`. Ran unit tests: `pnpm exec vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts -t "rawBody hook gating" --reporter=verbose`.

Evidence after fix:
```
Case A — Channel happy path (Telegram DM):
[rawbody-probe] runId    = 4c1c5a9b-8e2c-405f-b6f3-d2246e4912cc
[rawbody-probe] trigger  = user
[rawbody-probe] rawBody  = "hello rawbody test 004"   (22 chars)
[rawbody-probe] lastUser = "Conversation info (untrusted metadata):\n...
                            hello rawbody test 003"   (411 chars)
[rawbody-probe] equal    = false
[rawbody-probe] eventKeys = prompt,messages,rawBody

Case B — Heartbeat origin:
[rawbody-probe] runId    = 40d31407-6882-4f23-bdc7-85ba18fb5a41
[rawbody-probe] trigger  = heartbeat
[rawbody-probe] rawBody  = <undefined>
[rawbody-probe] lastUser = "[OpenClaw heartbeat poll]"
[rawbody-probe] eventKeys = messages,success,error,durationMs,rawBody,runId

Unit tests after fix (6/6 pass):
✓ passes user text as rawBody for telegram channel runs       (62ms)
✓ gates rawBody to undefined for heartbeat runs               (2ms)
✓ gates rawBody to undefined for cron-event runs              (1ms)
✓ gates rawBody to undefined for exec-event runs              (1ms)
✓ prefers CommandBody over Body for channel rawBody           (1ms)
✓ threads rawBody through steer path for non-telegram channels (3ms)
Test Files  1 passed
     Tests  6 passed
```

Observed result after fix: `event.rawBody` on `before_prompt_build` and `agent_end` contains the exact 22-char clean user input for channel runs. Non-channel origins correctly emit `undefined`. The steer path unit test confirms `followupRun.rawBody` is threaded through for non-Telegram channels (discord provider).

What was not tested: Live steer-race reproduction on Discord/Slack/WhatsApp channels (Telegram's `serializeImmediate: true` prevents triggering the steer path live). The steer fix is covered by the new unit test which mocks `isEmbeddedPiRunStreaming = true` and asserts `rawBody` propagation. No multi-user concurrency test was performed.

target.title: External reranker
target.body:
## Summary What problem does this PR solve? OpenClaw's memory-core has no support for external rerankers whe using memorySearch:query:hybrid — only the built-in MMR algorithm or switching the entire system to QMD. QMD is local-only, so the reranker runs on the gateway. This change allows users to configure an external reranker with memory-core. Why does this matter now? It increases the quality of results from memory searches. As internal memories grow, ensuring the best results becomes more important. What is the intended outcome? Better results from memory searches. What is intentionally out of scope? QMD, LanceDB, and other memory systems for Openclaw. What does success look like? Subtle, but important, the user doesn't notice any difference other than "Openclaw is better at finding information in memories". What should reviewers focus on? Look at the interface used between memory-core and MMR. MMR has been moved to a plugin, and the new external reranker uses the same interface.
Summary guidance This PR makes two changes. First, it moves mmr to a bundled plugin called memory-mmr. Second, it adds a memory-external-reranker plugin to memory-core, enabling external reranker support for Cohere-compatible /v1/rerank endpoints. It is designed so that users with existing memorySearch:query:hybrid configuruations see no difference and don't have to update their configuration to continue to use the existing mmr. Only when the user enables and memory-external-reranker plugin and adds the other necessary config entries will the external reranker be used. it's also designed so the user can configure mmr as a fallback if the external reranker fails for any reason (plugin not loaded, external reranker call fails).
## Linked context Which issue does this close? Closes #64438 Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related? Related # Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?
Linked context guidance https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/64438
## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: external reranker called during memory recall - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Logs from a running OpenClaw instance showing the external reranker in action: ``` 13:09:30+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] reranking with provider=local-llm-server-02-8082 model=mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 fallbacks=none topN=30 documents=34 13:09:30+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 model=mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 url=http://local-llm-server-02:8082/v1/rerank topN=30 documents=34 ``` ... ``` 13:09:36+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 response status=200 ok=true 13:09:36+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 success: 30 documents reranked ``` The reranker plugin loaded as a gateway plugin, received 34 candidate documents, sent them to the remote endpoint, and returned 30 reranked results. - Observed result after fix: better search results from memory - What was not tested: QMD, LanceDB, other memory systems - Proof limitations or environment constraints: - Before evidence (optional but encouraged):
Real behavior proof guidance config json fragments agents:defaults: ``` "memorySearch": { "enabled": true, "provider": "openai-compatible", "model": "Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "query": { "hybrid": { "enabled": true, "vectorWeight": 0.7, "textWeight": 0.3, "mmr": { "enabled": true, "lambda": 0.7, "provider": "memory-external-reranker", "fallback": "memory-mmr" } } }, "remote": { "baseUrl": "http://local-llm-server-02:8081/v1", "apiKey": { "source": "env", "provider": "default", "id": "LOCAL_LLM_02_KEY" }, "batch": { "enabled": false }, "nonBatchConcurrency": 3 }, } ``` models:providers: ``` "local-llm-server-02-8082": { "baseUrl": "http://local-llm-server-02:8082", "models": [ { "id": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "name": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "input": [ "text" ] } ] } ``` plugins:entries: ``` "memory-external-reranker": { "enabled": true, "config": { "provider": "local-llm-server-02-8082", "model": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "topN": 30 } }, ``` - Real environment tested: multiple test VMs. I've been running it on my production Openclaw instance for over a week. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: Enable and configure the memory-external-reranker plugin, index memory, perform a memory search like "What do you remember about the Lucid Air?" See attached screenshot.
## Tests and validation Which commands did you run? pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test pnpm test:extension memory-external-reranker pnpm test:extension memory-mmr What regression coverage was added or updated? Full tests added for both MMR plugin and new external reranker plugin. What failed before this fix, if known? If no test was added, why not?
Testing guidance To do full testing, an embedding model and reranking model are needed. I use llamacpp on localhost and update openclaw.json to point to the local models. ``` ./llama-server -hf Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B-GGUF --port 8081 --embedding ./llama-server -hf ggml-org/Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B-Q8_0-GGUF --port 8082 --embedding --reranking --pooling rank ```
## Risk checklist Did user-visible behavior change? No Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? Yes. New config entries added Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No. Uses existing patterns and code to call external reranker. What is the highest-risk area? Memory search results are not reranked by either the external reranker nor MMR. How is that risk mitigated? MMR is a bundled plugin. As long as mmr is a buldled plugin and loaded by default, the risk is minimal.
Risk guidance Minimal risk once MMR is a bundled plugin.
## Current review state What is the next action? What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
Review state guidance Keep this as the durable state for review progress. If useful information appears in comments, fold the current next action or blocker back here so maintainers and ClawSweeper do not need to reconstruct state from comment history.

target.title: fix(proxy): apply enhanced NO_PROXY matching to global undici dispatcher
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When `HTTPS_PROXY` is set in the environment, `ensureGlobalUndiciEnvProxyDispatcher()` installs a global undici `EnvHttpProxyAgent` that routes ALL HTTP requests through the proxy — including internal plugin requests that should bypass it via NO_PROXY.

The built-in undici `EnvHttpProxyAgent` NO_PROXY matcher does not support:
- **Leading-dot subdomain patterns** like `.myqcloud.com`
- **CIDR notation** like `100.64.0.0/10`
- **Octet wildcards** like `100.64.*`

OpenClaw already has an enhanced `matchesNoProxy()` function, but it was only used by provider HTTP helpers — NOT by the global dispatcher.

### Fix (revised per ClawSweeper review)

1. **NO_PROXY-aware dispatcher wrapper**: Wrap the global EnvHttpProxyAgent so each dispatch checks OpenClaw's enhanced `matchesNoProxy()` before routing through the proxy.

2. **ALL_PROXY kept SSRF-safe**: Unlike the previous approach, `hasEnvHttpProxyConfigured()` (the SSRF-safe shared helper) stays HTTP(S)-only. The global dispatcher uses `hasEnvHttpProxyAgentConfigured()` directly, which correctly includes ALL_PROXY via `resolveEnvHttpProxyAgentOptions()`. This is safe because the global dispatcher already runs inside the EnvHttpProxyAgent.

3. **All construction paths wrapped**: `createNoProxyAwareEnvDispatcher()` is now applied in all three places that build an EnvHttpProxyAgent: bootstrap (`ensureGlobalUndiciEnvProxyDispatcher`), timeout hardening (`applyGlobalDispatcherStreamTimeouts`), and proxy lifecycle reset (`forceResetGlobalDispatcher`).

## Evidence

### Test matrix

| Test file | Tests | Result |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| `proxy-env.test.ts` | 55 | ✅ Passed |
| `undici-global-dispatcher.test.ts` | 33 | ✅ Passed |
| **Total** | **88** | **✅ All passed** |

### Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Global undici dispatcher respects enhanced NO_PROXY matching (subdomain/CIDR/wildcard). ALL_PROXY deployments use the correct proxy routing without widening SSRF guards. Proxy lifecycle resets preserve the enhanced bypass.

- **Real environment tested**: Local source checkout, Node.js v24.13.1, Linux x64

- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
  Tests  88 passed (88)
  proxy-env — 55 tests (ALL_PROXY still ignored by SSRF guard ✅)
  undici-global-dispatcher — 33 tests (reset path wrapped ✅)
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: ALL_PROXY-only environments route through the proxy. SSRF-guarded fetch paths stay HTTP(S)-only. All EnvHttpProxyAgent construction paths carry the enhanced wrapper. NO_PROXY bypass with subdomain/CIDR/wildcard works.

- **What was not tested**: End-to-end with a real proxy and qqbot COS upload (requires live credentials). The enhanced matching is covered by unit tests.

### Checks
- Format: ✅
- Diff: +15 / -10 (25 lines, <=300)
- Branch: clean from origin/main
- Lockfile: unchanged
- SSRF boundary: preserved (HTTP(S)-only)

target.title: feat(agents): add per-agent env subprocess contract
target.body:
Closes #93425

## What Problem This Solves

Resolves a problem where `agents.list[]` can define agent-specific behavior but had no supported config contract for passing safe per-agent environment variables into the subprocesses that actually run that agent's CLI, exec, ACP, ACPX, or stdio MCP work.

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Operators need a single `agents.list[].env` entry to affect the real spawned process boundaries for that agent, not just an in-memory agent config object.
- **Root cause:** The OpenClaw agent config schema and resolver did not expose an `env` contract, so CLI runner, exec tools, ACP session creation/reuse, ACPX session options, and stdio MCP transport creation had no shared source-of-truth for per-agent environment overrides.
- **Why it matters / User impact:** Per-agent tools and runtimes can require stable environment variables for SDKs, credentials selectors, feature toggles, or integration probes; without a canonical config path, each subprocess boundary behaved differently.

## Why This Change Was Made

What is the intended outcome?

Root-cause fix: add `agents.list[].env` as the canonical string-map config contract and route its sanitized value from `resolveAgentConfig` into each real subprocess boundary that executes agent work.

What should reviewers focus on?

Review the source-of-truth path from `agents.list[].env` through `resolveAgentConfig`, then into CLI runner env assembly, exec env assembly, ACP initialize/re-ensure inputs, ACPX `SessionAgentOptions.env`, stdio MCP transport env, and `tools.effective` MCP summary fingerprinting.

What is intentionally out of scope?

No default environment variables were added, no migration is needed for existing configs, no server-specific MCP `env` precedence changed, no host env safety policy was relaxed, and no unrelated provider/auth/session behavior is changed.

- **Fix classification:** Root-cause fix for a missing public config/schema/protocol contract and its downstream subprocess propagation paths.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The patch fixes the contract source and every consumer boundary that spawns or reuses agent subprocess work; it does not add a one-off fallback at a single runner. MCP catalog fingerprints also include the same session-derived env so `tools.effective` projects the warm runtime instead of masking an env-scoped catalog as stale.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** `resolveAgentConfig(cfg, agentId)` remains the source-of-truth owner for per-agent options. `agents.list[].env` is copied out of that resolver and sanitized at process boundaries with the existing host-env security policy.
- **Patch quality notes:** Patch quality warnings are expected from small guard returns in ACPX model-capability error detection and test-only config casts. These are not fallback masking; they keep behavior explicit while the env contract remains owned by config schema/resolver and subprocess boundary sanitization.
- **Related open PR / canonical PR scan:** ACPX #389 is already merged and exposes `SessionAgentOptions.env`; this PR consumes that upstream contract in OpenClaw rather than competing with it. The downstream scope is OpenClaw #93425.
- **What did NOT change:** Out of scope: existing configs without `agents.list[].env` behave the same; dangerous `NODE_OPTIONS`, loader/preload, shell-hook, and PATH-style overrides remain ignored; MCP server-local `env` still wins over inherited agent env; request/plugin/channel exec env precedence remains unchanged.
- **Risk labels considered:** public config/schema/protocol, compatibility, session-state, security-boundary, availability.
- **Risk explanation:** The highest merge risk is security-boundary and compatibility risk because env propagation touches child processes and session/tool runtime reuse. The change is opt-in, string-only, and uses OpenClaw's existing sanitizer instead of introducing a new allow/deny policy.
- **Why acceptable:** Safe env keys are forwarded only where the selected agent's work is already spawned; dangerous startup overrides are filtered before child processes; tests cover direct subprocess, exec, ACP, ACPX, stdio MCP, and `tools.effective` warm-catalog behavior.

## User Impact

Operators can configure safe per-agent environment variables once on the agent entry and have them show up consistently when that agent starts CLI subprocesses, runs exec tools, initializes/restarts ACP sessions, starts ACPX-backed sessions, or launches stdio MCP servers.

Dangerous startup overrides such as `NODE_OPTIONS` and `PATH` are not forwarded through this new contract.

## Evidence

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/server-methods/tools-effective.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.resolve-env-hook.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.initialize-session.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.runtime-handles.test.ts extensions/acpx/src/runtime.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/tools-effective.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`
- Local child-process proof script, with copied output below
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-post-main-sync.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src-post-main-sync.tsbuildinfo`
- `git diff --check`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Config schema accepts `agents.list[].env` string maps and rejects non-string values.
- Agent resolver returns env only for the selected agent.
- CLI runner and exec subprocess env include safe agent env and drop blocked startup overrides.
- ACP initialize and runtime re-ensure paths pass sanitized env into runtime session inputs.
- ACPX runtime maps OpenClaw env into `SessionAgentOptions.env`.
- Stdio MCP transport inherits sanitized agent env while preserving server-local env precedence.
- Session MCP runtime fingerprints include sanitized inherited env.
- `tools.effective` passes `sessionKey` into the MCP summary and includes `sessionKey`/`agentId` in its summary cache key, so warm env-scoped MCP catalogs are not misreported as stale.

What failed before this fix, if known?

Before the fix, there was no schema/resolver contract for `agents.list[].env`; after the MCP fingerprint hardening, the RED `tools.effective` regression showed an env-scoped warm catalog being hidden as stale when the read-only summary omitted `sessionKey`.

- **Target test file:** `src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts`, `src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts`, `src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts`, `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.resolve-env-hook.test.ts`, `src/acp/control-plane/manager.initialize-session.test.ts`, `src/acp/control-plane/manager.runtime-handles.test.ts`, `extensions/acpx/src/runtime.test.ts`, `src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts`, `src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`, and `src/gateway/server-methods/tools-effective.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** A selected agent defines safe env plus dangerous `NODE_OPTIONS`/`PATH`; safe env reaches real child processes and dangerous overrides stay absent. A warm MCP runtime with an env-scoped fingerprint remains visible in `tools.effective` instead of producing `mcp-stale-catalog`.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The tests cover each subprocess boundary touched by the public contract and the live proof starts real spawned child processes; broader gateway startup proof would not prove this non-startup env propagation issue.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran focused Vitest shards for config/agent/CLI/exec/ACP/ACPX/MCP/`tools.effective`, ran session MCP runtime regression, ran TypeScript checks, ran whitespace diff check, and ran a local proof script that spawned direct and stdio MCP child processes.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/server-methods/tools-effective.test.ts
Test Files: 2 passed
Tests: 42 passed

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.resolve-env-hook.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.initialize-session.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.runtime-handles.test.ts extensions/acpx/src/runtime.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/tools-effective.test.ts
[test] passed 6 Vitest shards in 421.28s
Test Files: 10 passed
Tests: 267 passed

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts
Test Files: 1 passed
Tests: 33 passed

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-post-main-sync.tsbuildinfo
Result: passed

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src-post-main-sync.tsbuildinfo
Result: passed

git diff --check
Result: passed
```

- **Observed result after fix:**

```text
schema accepts agents.list[].env string maps: yes
agent config resolves env for selected agent: yes
direct spawned child OPENCLAW_AGENT_ENV=visible-to-spawned-child
direct spawned child NODE_OPTIONS=<absent>
direct spawned child PATH override=<absent>
MCP stdio spawned child OPENCLAW_AGENT_ENV=visible-to-spawned-child
MCP stdio spawned child NODE_OPTIONS=<absent>
MCP stdio spawned child PATH override=<absent>
```

- **What was not tested:** I did not use live external provider credentials or perform a production deployment. The validation is focused on the config contract, subprocess env propagation, MCP runtime/catalog behavior, TypeScript correctness, and real local child-process behavior.

What is the next action?

Ready for maintainer review after the listed focused regressions, type checks, diff check, live child-process proof, and local ClawSweeper commit review found no actionable issues.

target.title: improve: skip Gateway migration preflight without legacy state
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`openclaw gateway run` paid the full doctor state-migration preflight on startup even when there was no legacy state to migrate. In the generic Gateway startup benchmark this kept the current-main hot path dominated by legacy state migration/import work and jiti/module-loading overhead.

## Why This Change Was Made

This change gates the Gateway foreground startup doctor migration preflight behind lightweight legacy-input detection, matching the existing read-only command behavior while preserving automatic migration for known legacy sources.

The detector covers:

- core state sidecars and pending SQLite archives
- bundled channel legacy state
- delivery queue, debug-proxy, config-health/update/current-conversation state
- cron legacy JSON/state/run-log stores, including configured `cron.store`
- shipped plugin doctor migration sources (Active Memory, Phone Control, ACPX, Device Pair, Matrix, Nostr, MSTeams, Voice Call, Memory Core, Memory Wiki, Workboard, Telegram)
- configured session stores and config-derived plugin paths
- conservative fallback when the shared-state SQLite probe cannot run

It also avoids persistent false positives from already-archived `*.migrated` plugin sources and normal current plugin-state rows.

## User Impact

Generic Gateway cold startup is substantially faster when no legacy migration input exists. Users with legacy state still get the same automatic startup migration path as before.

Benchmark artifact:

- `.artifacts/gateway-startup-upstream/20260624T001328Z-gateway-preflight-final-candidate/before-after-summary.md`

P50 Gateway-ready deltas vs `origin/main` baseline:

| case | baseline p50 ready ms | candidate p50 ready ms | delta |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| default | 3380.7 | 1882.3 | -1498.4ms (-44.3%) |
| skipChannels | 3087.7 | 1393.6 | -1694.1ms (-54.9%) |
| oneInternalHook | 3060.8 | 1395.7 | -1665.1ms (-54.4%) |
| allInternalHooks | 3056.5 | 1391.3 | -1665.2ms (-54.5%) |
| fiftyPlugins | 3291.8 | 1587.5 | -1704.3ms (-51.8%) |
| fiftyStartupLazyPlugins | 3170.3 | 1391.3 | -1779.0ms (-56.1%) |

CPU profile evidence after the change:

- `.artifacts/gateway-startup-upstream/20260624T001534Z-gateway-preflight-final-cpu/cpu/cpu-self-summary.md`
- skipChannels one-run: Gateway-ready 1415.8ms, CPU 2470ms
- top-profile keyword self-time: `jiti=0.0ms`, `state-migrations=0.0ms`; remaining top self-time is mostly Node package-json reads / process spawn.

## Evidence

Passed:

- `pnpm test src/cli/argv.test.ts src/cli/program/config-guard.test.ts`
  - 131 argv tests + 65 config-guard tests passed
- `pnpm build:docker`
- `pnpm check`
- `codex review --base origin/main`
  - no blocking regression identified after reviewer-driven fixes
- `pnpm test:startup:gateway --runs 5 --warmup 1 --output .artifacts/gateway-startup-upstream/20260624T001328Z-gateway-preflight-final-candidate/gateway-startup.json`
- `pnpm test:startup:gateway --case skipChannels --runs 1 --warmup 0 --cpu-prof-dir .artifacts/gateway-startup-upstream/20260624T001534Z-gateway-preflight-final-cpu/cpu --output .artifacts/gateway-startup-upstream/20260624T001534Z-gateway-preflight-final-cpu/gateway-startup.json`

Validation caveat:

- Full `pnpm test` was run and failed in unrelated shards outside this patch's two changed files. Failures included provider catalog expectations, timing/process tests, backup late-SQLite tests, security scan allowlist tests, and script resolver tests. The failure log is `.artifacts/gateway-startup-upstream/pnpm-test-gateway-preflight-final.log`.

target.title: fix: refresh status context window after model switch
target.body:
## Summary
- Ignore stale persisted contextTokens when /status sees a selected/default model change that is not an active fallback.
- Preserve equivalent runtime aliases while doing the stale-context check, so CLI runtime aliases keep their live context window and auth display.
- Drop cached contextTokens from the temporary live model identity used by session_status(current).
- Add regression coverage for default model switches and live current-session status.

Related to #10278, #14969, and #70692.

## Test plan
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/auto-reply/status.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.session-status.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/status/status-message.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: `/status` reused a stale persisted `contextTokens` value after the selected/default model changed, so a 1m-window selected model could render as `used/200k` until the next chat turn refreshed runtime state.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on branch `codex/fix-status-stale-context-window`, using the same status renderer that backs the live `/status` output and a redacted session shape matching the reported `siderclaw-yc` stale-session state.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran `node --import tsx --eval '<buildStatusMessage with selected sider-litellm-messages/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6, stale active sider-litellm-completions/deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash, totalTokens=10000, contextTokens=200000>'` from the PR checkout.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): copied terminal output after the patch:

```text
🧠 Model: sider-litellm-messages/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 · 🔑 api-key
📚 Context: 10k/1.0m (1%) · 🧹 Compactions: 0
```

target.title: fix(telegram): suppress fallback reply when plugin command returns suppressReply: true
target.body:
## Summary

Plugin commands registered via `api.registerCommand` that send responses directly via Telegram Bot API (with custom retry logic, IPv4 forcing, etc.) currently trigger a spurious "No response generated. Please try again." fallback message from the Telegram channel adapter. This happens because the adapter's `hasRenderableTelegramNativeReplyPayload` check only looks for `text`/`mediaUrl` content, and when the handler returns `undefined` or `{}` after doing its own delivery, no content is found so the fallback fires.

This PR adds `suppressReply?: boolean` to `PluginCommandResult` so handlers can explicitly signal "I already took care of delivery — don't send a fallback."

## Changes

- `src/plugins/types.ts`: Add `suppressReply?: boolean` to `PluginCommandResult` with detailed JSDoc explaining when and why plugin authors should use it (for commands that deliver via channel-native APIs with custom transport guarantees)
- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts`: Add `isSuppressedTelegramNativeReplyPayload` check before the fallback path; clean up progress placeholder and return early when suppressed
- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.test.ts`: Regression test ensuring `{ suppressReply: true }` produces no `deliverReplies` or `editMessageTelegram` calls

Fixes #80756

## Real Behavior Proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Plugin commands that own their own transport delivery via Telegram Bot API produce a spurious "No response generated. Please try again." fallback because the handler returns no content and the adapter cannot distinguish "handled externally" from "no response".

**Real environment tested:** macOS 15.4, Node v26.0.0, local OpenClaw checkout on branch fix/80756-suppress-plugin-command-reply.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
1. Cloned openclaw/openclaw main, created branch fix/80756-suppress-plugin-command-reply
2. Added `suppressReply?: boolean` to `PluginCommandResult` type in `src/plugins/types.ts` with JSDoc documenting the flag
3. Added `isSuppressedTelegramNativeReplyPayload` helper and early-return guard in `extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts`
4. Added regression test in `extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.test.ts`
5. Ran the full test suite for the affected file

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from running the Telegram bot-native-commands test suite locally:

```
$ pnpm vitest run extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.6 /private/tmp/openclaw-main

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  19 passed (19)
   Start at  23:37:51
   Duration  3.49s (transform 1.29s, setup 90ms, import 3.09s, tests 218ms, environment 0ms)
```

All 19 tests passed, including the new regression test for `suppressReply: true`.

**Observed result after fix:**
- Plugin command handlers returning `{ suppressReply: true }` no longer trigger the EMPTY_RESPONSE_FALLBACK path in Telegram native commands.
- The progress placeholder is cleaned up correctly before the early return.
- Existing behavior for handlers returning `{ text: "..." }`, `{}`, or `undefined` is unchanged.
- Commit `eb3bf1a0` pushed to `fix/80756-suppress-plugin-command-reply` with the JSDoc update addressing the ClawSweeper P3 finding.

**What was not tested:** No live Telegram Bot API integration or actual message delivery was exercised; only the unit test harness behavior was validated. The fix is a pure TypeScript type + control-flow change with no runtime dependency on external APIs.

## Maintainer notes

ClawSweeper review flagged the need for live Telegram proof. This change is a pure SDK API addition + Telegram adapter control-flow fix. The regression test covers the exact code path. If a maintainer believes the test coverage is sufficient, applying `@clawsweeper re-review` or the `proof: override` label would allow this to proceed.

target.title: fix(compaction): make overflow tuning configurable
target.body:
## Summary

- add `agents.defaults.compaction.preemptiveOverflowRatio` for Pi tool-loop preemptive overflow threshold tuning
- add `agents.defaults.compaction.maxOverflowAttempts` for Pi overflow compaction retry tuning
- preserve current defaults (`0.9` ratio, `3` overflow retries) and document schema labels/help text

Fixes #80638
Fixes #80639

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Pi compaction overflow tuning is now configurable instead of hardcoded: configured values are accepted by the config schema and used by runtime resolver helpers, while unset config preserves the old defaults.
- **Real environment tested:** Harry's Mac mini, Darwin 25.4.0 arm64, Node v22.22.0, local OpenClaw checkout on branch `fix/80638-80639-compaction-knobs`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node --import tsx - <<'EOF'
import { resolveMaxOverflowCompactionAttempts, resolvePreemptiveOverflowRatio } from './src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compaction-runtime-config.ts';
import { AgentDefaultsSchema } from './src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.ts';

const config = { agents: { defaults: { compaction: { preemptiveOverflowRatio: 0.72, maxOverflowAttempts: 1 } } } };
const parsed = AgentDefaultsSchema.parse(config.agents.defaults);
console.log('runtime preemptiveOverflowRatio:', resolvePreemptiveOverflowRatio(config));
console.log('runtime maxOverflowAttempts:', resolveMaxOverflowCompactionAttempts(config));
console.log('schema preserved preemptiveOverflowRatio:', parsed.compaction?.preemptiveOverflowRatio);
console.log('schema preserved maxOverflowAttempts:', parsed.compaction?.maxOverflowAttempts);
console.log('default preemptiveOverflowRatio:', resolvePreemptiveOverflowRatio({}));
console.log('default maxOverflowAttempts:', resolveMaxOverflowCompactionAttempts({}));
EOF
```

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the real local checkout:

```text
runtime preemptiveOverflowRatio: 0.72
runtime maxOverflowAttempts: 1
schema preserved preemptiveOverflowRatio: 0.72
schema preserved maxOverflowAttempts: 1
default preemptiveOverflowRatio: 0.9
default maxOverflowAttempts: 3
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Configured values (`0.72`, `1`) are parsed and resolved at runtime; default behavior remains `0.9` and `3` when unset.
- **What was not tested:** A live long-running overflow failure against a paid provider; validation used the real config/runtime code paths directly.

## Validation

- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compaction-runtime-config.test.ts src/config/config.compaction-settings.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts`
- `pnpm -s config:schema:check`
- `pnpm -s check:test-types`
